prompt:
You are a mathematical research advisor with access to web search. Your job is to orchestrate solver agents to assemble the best possible proof for the problem below. You have 10 advisor calls total (including this one). Plan strategically. Use web search whenever you need to look up specific results, verify claims, or find techniques.

# Problem
We consider infinite countable first-order structures, their automorphism groups and the descriptive aspects of their representations. A representation $\A$ of such a structure is an instance of its isomorphism type in which the universe or domain of the structure is the set of natural numbers $\N$ and the constants, functions and relations of the structure are elements of $\N$, multi-variable functions from $\N$ to $\N$ and multi-place relations on $\N$, respectively.   Similarly, an automorphism of $\A$ or an isomorphism from $\A$ to $\B$ is a bijective function from $\N$ to $\N$ that preserves the evaluation of all constants, functions and relations.  We let $\AUT(\A)$ denote the automorphism group of $\A$.

\begin{definition}\label{1.1}
  \begin{itemize}
  \item     $\A$ is \emph{computably $\AUT$-countable} if every automorphism of $\A$ is computable relative to $\A$.  
  \item  $\A$ is \emph{computably $\AUT$-countable on a cone} if there is a $C\subset\N$ such that whenever $\B$ is isomorphic to $\A$, every automorphism of $\B$ is computable relative to $\B\oplus C$.
  \end{itemize}
\end{definition}


\begin{definition}
  A function $f:\A\to\A$ is \emph{$\Sigma^{in}_1$-definable} in $\A$ relative to parameters
  $\bar{a}=(a_1,\dots,a_n)$ from $\A$ if there is a family of existential formulas $(\phi_i)_{i\in\N}$ such that for all $x$ and $y$ in $\A$, $f(x)=y$ if and only if there is an $i\in\N$ such that $\A\models\phi_i(\bar{a},x,y)$.
\end{definition}

\begin{question}\label{sec:question}\label{1.2}
  Is there a countable computably-represented structure $\A$ such that $\A$ is computably $\AUT$-countable on a cone and such that for any finite  set of parameters $\bar{a}$ in $\A$ there is an automorphism $\pi$ of $\A$ such that $\pi$ is not $\Sigma^{in}_1$-definable in $\A$ relative to the parameters $\bar{a}\cup \pi(\bar{a}),$ where $\pi(\bar{a})$ is the image of $\bar{a}$ under $\pi$.
\end{question}

# Strategic Directions (from literature survey)
The problem sits exactly at the gap between nonuniform relative computability and parameter-exact existential definability. The first crucial reduction is topological: computable AUT-countability on a cone implies Aut(A) is countable in the fixed computable copy, hence as a closed subgroup of S_infinity it is discrete and has a finite base b. Thus every automorphism is uniquely determined by the image of b. If one could show that the global action relation Act_b(q,x,y), meaning 'the unique automorphism sending b to q sends x to y', is Sigma^in_1-definable over one fixed finite tuple, then the requested example cannot exist. This is the most promising line, and it should be attacked using the intrinsic definability and existential-atomicity machinery from Degree Spectra of Relations on a Cone, https://arxiv.org/abs/1412.3842, Effectively Existentially-Atomic Structures, https://math.berkeley.edu/~antonio/papers/EAtomicStructures.pdf, and A Robuster Scott Rank, https://math.berkeley.edu/~antonio/papers/scottRank.pdf.

## Directions to Explore

### Finite-base reduction and global action relation
Start by proving the finite-base reduction rigorously. Since A is computable and all automorphisms of A are computable relative to A plus the cone base C, Aut(A) is countable. As a closed subgroup of S_infinity it is discrete, so there is a finite tuple b whose pointwise stabilizer is trivial. Define Act_b(q,x,y) in the expansion (A,b): q is in the orbit of b and y is the value at x of the unique automorphism taking b to q. If Act_b is Sigma^in_1-definable over b and one additional fixed finite tuple r, then taking a=b r refutes the desired example, because for every pi, Graph(pi)(x,y) is Act_b(pi(b),x,y), using only a union pi(a) as allowed parameters.
Key ideas: Reduce the problem from individual automorphism graphs to one global action relation indexed by finite images of a base.; Try to show that computable AUT-countability on a cone forces Act_b to be relatively intrinsically c.e. on a cone, or at least Sigma^in_1 after finitely many fixed parameters.; If this works, the answer to the original question is negative: choosing the finite base plus the fixed uniformizing parameters gives a finite parameter set over which every automorphism graph is Sigma^in_1-definable.
Relevant techniques: Effectively Closed Subgroups of the Infinite Symmetric Group, https://homepages.ecs.vuw.ac.nz/~melnikal/permutation%20groups.pdf: the introductory observation identifies Aut(A) of a computable structure as an effectively closed subgroup of S_infinity; Proposition 4.1 and the surrounding discrete-group analysis support the finite-base/discreteness reduction.; Computable Functors and Effective Interpretability, https://arxiv.org/abs/1506.02737: the quoted AKMS-Chisholm intrinsic definability theorem says uniform relative c.e. behavior gives computable infinitary Sigma^c_1 definitions; this is the exact bridge needed if Act_b can be made uniform.; Computability theoretic classifications for classes of structures, https://math.berkeley.edu/~antonio/papers/ClassesOfStructures.pdf: Theorem 3.10 gives r.i.c.e. iff computably infinitary Sigma^c_1 definability with finite parameters; the task is to force the parameters to be fixed once and for all.; Effectively Existentially-Atomic Structures, https://math.berkeley.edu/~antonio/papers/EAtomicStructures.pdf: Lemma 6.2 is the key finite-use/permutation extraction argument turning a uniform computable operator into existential support formulas.; Computable Polish Group Actions, https://math.berkeley.edu/~antonio/papers/balls.pdf: Lemma 16 and Theorem 17 give Baire-category and orbit-map tools for turning many pointwise computations into uniform information on a cone.
Potential obstacles: The hypothesis is strongly nonuniform: for each copy and each automorphism there may be a different Turing index, and that index may hardwire finitely many helper elements of the copy. Existing intrinsic definability theorems often allow extra finite parameters depending on the relation; the original question forbids extra parameters beyond a union pi(a). The hard point is to prove that all those hidden helper parameters can be replaced by one fixed finite tuple r.

### Parameter-restricted relation-spectrum contrapositive
Fix the finite base b and an automorphism pi. In the rigid expansion E_pi=(A,b,pi(b)), the graph of pi is an invariant binary relation. If it is not Sigma^in_1-definable without extra parameters in E_pi, try to build a copy of E_pi in which the transported graph is not computable from the copy plus the cone oracle. Such a copy would contradict computable AUT-countability. This would give a direct no-go theorem, but it requires a parameter-restricted strengthening of the usual relation-spectrum results.
Key ideas: Use b to make the graph of pi invariant: because b is a base, the expansion by b and pi(b) is rigid.; Adapt Harizanov/Ash-Nerode free-tuple coding so that no hidden finite parameters are allowed in the definition.; If non-Sigma^in_1 over b union pi(b) always yields a copy with noncomputable graph, then computable AUT-countability forces every pi to be definable over b union pi(b), refuting the desired example.
Relevant techniques: Degree Spectra of Relations on a Cone, https://arxiv.org/abs/1412.3842: Section 2.3 equates intrinsic Sigma^0_alpha on a cone with Sigma^in_alpha definability; Corollary 2.7 gives the c.e.-degree fullness dichotomy once a relation is not intrinsically computable on a cone.; Degree Spectra of Relations on a Cone, https://arxiv.org/abs/1412.3842: Theorem 5.1 and Proposition 5.4 supply 2-free tuple methods for turning failure of low definability into copies with higher relation degree.; A relation on (omega, <) of intermediate degree spectrum on a cone, https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.01071: Theorem 3.2 and the coding-sequence machinery show how to make parameter-sensitive finite-gadget coding robust on a cone.; A Robuster Scott Rank, https://math.berkeley.edu/~antonio/papers/scottRank.pdf: Lemma 3.2 gives the alpha=1 type-omitting tool for unsupported Pi^in_1 types.
Potential obstacles: The standard relation-spectrum theorems allow finite parameters in the infinitary definition, exactly the loophole exploited by the problem. A graph may be computable in every copy because the computation hardwires a pi-specific helper tuple, while still not being definable from b union pi(b). A new parameter-restricted spectrum theorem is needed; it may be false without using rigidity or uniqueness of the automorphism.

### Private-key counterexample construction
If the uniformization/no-go approach fails, try to construct A with a countable discrete automorphism group and a finite base b, but with automorphisms pi_n whose graphs require private finite key tuples k_n. The intended pattern is: Graph(pi_n) is Sigma^in_1-definable over b, k_n, pi_n(b), pi_n(k_n), and computable in every copy by hardwiring the image of k_n, but for every finite parameter set a there is some n whose key orbit is missed by a, and Graph(pi_n) is not Sigma^in_1-definable over a union pi_n(a).
Key ideas: Use a countable discrete group action, not independent local swaps; independent swaps have uncountable closure and cannot be the automorphism group of a countable structure with computably AUT-countability.; Build infinitely many key orbits, one for each automorphism or family of automorphisms, while keeping a finite base for the whole group.; Attach rigid gadgets whose relevant correspondence is existentially visible only when the matching key is named.; Make every automorphism individually computable in arbitrary copies by allowing the Turing machine for that automorphism to hardwire finitely many key images.
Relevant techniques: Effectively Closed Subgroups of the Infinite Symmetric Group, https://homepages.ecs.vuw.ac.nz/~melnikal/permutation%20groups.pdf: Lemma 4.3 realizes closed subgroups by orbit-relation structures; Lemma 4.4 and Proposition 2.3 give rigid-order blow-ups preserving automorphism groups while making structures computable.; Effectively Existentially-Atomic Structures, https://math.berkeley.edu/~antonio/papers/EAtomicStructures.pdf: Theorem 1.10 characterizes finite-parameter existential atomicity by Sigma^in_3 Scott sentences and computable categoricity on a cone; useful for designing gadgets that become simple only after naming a key.; On Optimal Scott Sentences of Finitely Generated Algebraic Structures, https://arxiv.org/abs/1702.06448, and Complexity of Scott Sentences, https://arxiv.org/abs/1807.02715: the finite-generation results warn that if a finite tuple term-generates the structure, automorphism graphs tend to become low-definable from the tuple and its image.; A Computable Functor from Graphs to Fields, https://arxiv.org/abs/1510.07322: Theorem 1.2 and Theorem 9.9 can transfer a graph-based construction to fields while preserving automorphism spectra, if a graph example is found.
Potential obstacles: The closure constraint on automorphism groups is severe: a direct sum of countably many independent finite switches closes to an uncountable product. Regular or Cayley-style countable group actions often make every automorphism graph Sigma^in_1 from a base and its image via a disjunction over words. The construction must avoid term-connectedness while still allowing all automorphisms to be computable in arbitrary presentations.

### Type-omitting and copy-switching attack on unsupported graph types
Develop a direct contrapositive: assume that for a finite base b and some pi, the graph type of pi over b union pi(b) is not existentially supported. Use a type-omitting construction inside a class of partial automorphism expansions to build a copy B of A and a conjugate automorphism pi^B whose graph is not computable from B plus the cone oracle. This would prove that computable AUT-countability forces existential support for all automorphism graphs.
Key ideas: View y=pi(x) as a relation between pointed tuple types in the expansion by b and pi(b).; If no Sigma^in_1 formula supports the corresponding Pi^in_1 information, omit or split that type in a new model/copy.; Diagonalize against all candidate B plus C computations of the automorphism by waiting for finite use and then switching to an existentially indistinguishable but graph-incompatible configuration.
Relevant techniques: A Robuster Scott Rank, https://math.berkeley.edu/~antonio/papers/scottRank.pdf: Lemma 3.2 omits unsupported Pi^in_alpha types in models of Pi^in_{alpha+1} sentences; the alpha=1 case is the relevant one.; Effectively Existentially-Atomic Structures, https://math.berkeley.edu/~antonio/papers/EAtomicStructures.pdf: Lemma 5.3 omits unsupported universal types in Pi_2 classes, and Section 4 turns existential support of universal types into orbit definitions.; Punctual Definability on Structures, https://math.berkeley.edu/~antonio/papers/coneFeb.pdf: Proposition 4.3 gives an omitted-orbit copy-switching diagonalization; Theorem 4.4 shows how infinite orbit choices defeat candidate procedures.; On the computability of optimal Scott sentences, https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.09626: the labelled bouquet/direct-limit constructions in Theorem 1.2 show how to keep finite stages isomorphic while separating the limits.
Potential obstacles: One must define the right Pi_2 class of expansions so that the reduct remains a copy of A and the extra relation really is an automorphism graph. Type omission may produce a model satisfying a weak theory but not isomorphic to A unless a sufficiently strong Scott or back-and-forth condition is built in. This direction is technically demanding but targets the exact model-theoretic obstruction.

### Analytic equivalence relation and cone-uniformization route
Code automorphisms, finite partial automorphisms, or pointed automorphism graphs as reals and study the relevant equivalence relation under conjugacy by copies. The goal is to show that if finite-parameter Sigma^in_1 definitions fail too often, then there is a perfect or high-rank family of automorphism patterns; the perfect-set coding methods should then produce a copy with an automorphism hyperarithmetic but not computable from the copy plus the cone oracle, contradicting computable AUT-countability.
Key ideas: Treat nondefinable automorphism graphs as an analytic family of inequivalent patterns.; If the family has a perfect set, code a noncomputable real into a graph or automorphism as in the Vaught-conjecture and analytic-equivalence-relation papers.; If there is no perfect set, use ranked equivalence relations with countable rank fibers to obtain cone uniformity.
Relevant techniques: A Computability Theoretic Equivalent to Vaught's Conjecture, https://arxiv.org/abs/1206.5682: Theorem 2.2 on scattered projective ranked equivalence relations, Section 5.1 perfect-set coding into back-and-forth types, and the cone-extraction techniques are directly relevant.; Analytic equivalence relations satisfying hyperarithmetic-is-recursive, https://arxiv.org/abs/1306.1513: Theorem 1.3, Lemma 2.1 Borel approximations, and Lemma 2.3 Barwise compactness plus overspill give the general hyperarithmetic-is-recursive on a cone template.; Effectively Closed Subgroups of the Infinite Symmetric Group, https://homepages.ecs.vuw.ac.nz/~melnikal/permutation%20groups.pdf: Proposition 4.1 gives hyperarithmetic orbit-equivalence for discrete effectively closed groups.; Computable Polish Group Actions, https://math.berkeley.edu/~antonio/papers/balls.pdf: Theorem 17 connects G_delta orbits and uniform computability on a cone via Effros's theorem.
Potential obstacles: The object 'the graph of pi over a union pi(a)' is not invariant under arbitrary isomorphisms unless a contains a base or the stabilizer centralizes pi. Descriptive-set-theoretic cone theorems may yield hyperarithmetic or threshold-spectrum conclusions, but the original problem asks for exact Sigma^in_1 definability with a prescribed finite parameter set.

## Key Obstacles
The main barrier is nonuniformity. Computably AUT-countable on a cone says every individual automorphism of every copy is computable from that copy plus a fixed oracle, but the computing program may depend on the automorphism and on the copy and may hardwire finitely many unnamed elements. Standard AKMS-Chisholm intrinsic definability converts such behavior into Sigma^c_1 definitions only with extra finite parameters, while the question allows only a union pi(a). A second obstacle is invariance: the graph of a single automorphism is not an invariant relation in an unpointed structure; it becomes invariant only after naming a finite base and the image of that base. A third obstacle is the topology of Aut(A): countability forces discreteness and a finite base, ruling out easy independent-switch constructions because their closure is uncountable. Finally, many natural countable-group actions are too term-connected; once a finite base and its image are named, the automorphism graph is often a countable disjunction over words or terms and hence Sigma^in_1.

## Recommended Starting Point
Start with the finite-base/global-action route. Prove explicitly that Aut(A) is countable and hence has a finite base b. Then define Act_b(q,x,y) and try to prove the key lemma: if every section q |-> Act_b(q,.,.) is computable in every copy relative to the cone oracle, then Act_b is Sigma^in_1-definable over b plus one fixed finite tuple r. The first concrete step is to attempt a contraposition using the finite-use argument of Effectively Existentially-Atomic Structures, Lemma 6.2, and the type-omitting lemma of A Robuster Scott Rank, Lemma 3.2. If the lemma is true, the original question has a negative answer by taking a=b r. If it fails, the failure should exhibit exactly the private-key mechanism needed for a counterexample construction.

# Past Attempts on This Problem (from previous runs)


# Your Role
You are the sole orchestrator. You will:
1. Maintain a priority task list and a shared knowledge base.
2. Each round, assign 1–2 tasks to solver agents (parallel API calls).
3. Decide exactly what context each solver needs — you write their full prompts verbatim.
4. When you want a solver (or write-up agent) to read the **full text** of a prior    solver's output (e.g., to critique, attack, extend, or assemble it), put those task    IDs in `reference_task_ids` — the harness will auto-prepend the full output verbatim.    Do NOT try to paste prior outputs inline; just reference them by ID.
5. When significant partial results emerge, flag them for a dedicated write-up agent    (you write that prompt too, including all proof details found so far).

Note: you do NOT need to assemble a final solution yourself. When the budget runs out, a separate assembly stage will automatically synthesise the best possible solution from the shared KB. Focus every round on *exploration and proving new results*.

# Output Format
Reasoning prose first, then your plan inside an <ADVISOR_PLAN> tag. The content between the tags must be a single valid JSON object — do NOT wrap it in a markdown code block.

<ADVISOR_PLAN>
{
  "reasoning": "your strategic thinking — what to try first and why",
  "strategic_note": "2-3 sentence note for your future self: what you learned this round, what you decided, and why. This will be shown to you in every subsequent round as a memory aid.",
  "kb_updates": {
    "new_proven_results": [],
    "new_failed_attempts": [],
    "new_bottlenecks": [],
    "frontier": "the most important thing to establish next"
  },
  "task_assignments": [
    {
      "task_id": "t1",
      "description": "one-line label for logging",
      "reference_task_ids": [],
      "solver_prompt": "THE COMPLETE PROMPT to send verbatim to this solver. Include the problem statement, relevant KB results, proof technique hints, and precise instructions. Use `reference_task_ids` to pull in any prior solver outputs you want this solver to read in full — do NOT paste them inline here."
    }
  ],
  "writeup_tasks": [
    {
      "statement": "exact statement of the result being written up",
      "solves_original_problem": false,
      "reference_task_ids": [],
      "writeup_agent_prompt": "complete prompt for the write-up agent"
    }
  ],
  "action": "continue"
}
</ADVISOR_PLAN>

**reference_task_ids:** list of prior task IDs whose full outputs should be auto-attached to the agent's prompt. Strict for solver tasks (unknown IDs cause the task to fail) and lenient for writeups (unknown IDs are skipped with a warning). On round 1 there are no prior tasks, so leave this empty.

**solves_original_problem (writeup_tasks only):** set to `true` ONLY when the `statement` is the full original problem (not a relaxation, not a lemma, not a partial). When `true` and the resulting write-up passes verify+refine, the assembly stage will reuse the verified write-up verbatim instead of spending a fresh solver call. Set to `false` for any partial result, lemma, or relaxation. This flag is also used by the verifier to pick its framing: `true` ⇒ verify against the original problem verbatim; `false` ⇒ verify against the write-up's stated claim. Setting it incorrectly will give you a misleading verification verdict, so be honest about what is actually being written up.

**writeup_agent_prompt (writeup_tasks only):** the write-up agent will be evaluated by an independent referee, and the resulting artifact then flows downstream into benchmark and LaTeX-typesetting stages, none of which have access to this conversation, the shared KB, the solver outputs, or any custom notation introduced along the way. Your prompt MUST therefore instruct the agent to produce a **fully self-contained artifact**: (1) state the exact claim being proved up front, with every symbol, hypothesis, quantifier, and notational convention defined inline; (2) write the proof so a reader who has never seen the solver history, the KB, or this conversation can follow it end-to-end; (3) avoid any reference to "the problem above", "the previous result", "the construction we used", or other shorthand that depends on context the referee cannot see. If the agent needs concrete prior outputs to build on, list them in `reference_task_ids` (which auto-attaches them verbatim) — do NOT assume inherited context.

**action values:** `"continue"` — keep exploring; `"done"` — no more work needed.

Assign at most 2 solver tasks this round. Start with the highest-priority direction.

response:
Yes—the candidate works. I see no structural flaw. The only small clarifications I would make are: declare all relations false outside their intended sorts, and in the copy-computation algorithm search from **both** hardwired roots of a toggled \(S(l)\)-gadget, not just one.

## Construction

Use the finite relational language
\[
\{L,U,V,N,C,P,S,T\},
\]
where \(L,U,V\) are unary, \(N,P\) are binary, and \(C,S,T\) are ternary.

Let
\[
L=\omega,\qquad U=[\omega]^{<\omega},\qquad V=\omega\times 2\times \omega.
\]
Write \(P_l=\{(l,i,k):i<2,\ k\in\omega\}\), and let
\[
R_{l,i}=\{(l,i,k):k\in\omega\}.
\]
Define:
- \(N(l,m)\iff m=l+1\).
- \(C(l,u,v)\iff v=u\triangle\{l\}\).
- \(P(l,x)\iff x=(l,i,k)\) for some \(i,k\).
- \(S(l,(l,i,k),(l,i,k+1))\) for \(i<2,k\in\omega\).
- \(T(l,u,(l,i,k))\iff i=\mathbf 1_{l\in u}\).

All relations are false on wrongly sorted tuples.

For finite \(F\subseteq \omega\), define
\[
\alpha_F(l)=l,\qquad
\alpha_F(u)=u\triangle F,\qquad
\alpha_F(l,i,k)=(l,i\oplus \mathbf 1_{l\in F},k).
\]

---

## (1) Computability

Use a standard computable tagged coding:
- \(L\)-elements are \(\langle 0,n\rangle\).
- \(U\)-elements are \(\langle 1,e\rangle\), where \(e\) codes the finite set whose characteristic function is the binary expansion of \(e\).
- \(V\)-elements are \(\langle 2,l,i,k\rangle\), with \(i<2\).

Then all sort predicates are decidable. Also:
\[
C(l,e,e')\iff e'= e\oplus 2^l,
\]
and
\[
T(l,e,(l,i,k))\iff i=\text{the }l\text{th bit of }e.
\]
The relations \(N,P,S\) are plainly decidable from the tuple coordinates. Hence this is a computable presentation of \(A\).

---

## (2) Automorphism group

First, every \(\alpha_F\) is an automorphism. The label sort \(L\) and \(N\) are fixed. For \(C\),
\[
(u\triangle F)\triangle\{l\}=(u\triangle\{l\})\triangle F.
\]
For \(S\), the level \(k\) is preserved and, for each fixed \(l\), either both rays are fixed or both are swapped. For \(T\),
\[
\mathbf 1_{l\in u\triangle F}
=
\mathbf 1_{l\in u}\oplus \mathbf 1_{l\in F},
\]
exactly matching the side flip in \(V\).

Conversely, let \(\beta\in\operatorname{Aut}(A)\).

### Labels are fixed

Inside \(L\), the element \(0\) is the unique \(L\)-element with no \(N\)-predecessor. Hence \(\beta(0)=0\). If \(\beta(n)=n\), then \(\beta(n+1)\) is the unique \(N\)-successor of \(n\), so \(\beta(n+1)=n+1\). Thus \(\beta\) fixes \(L\) pointwise.

### The \(U\)-action is translation by a finite set

For each \(l\), let \(\tau_l(u)=u\triangle\{l\}\). Since \(C(l,u,\tau_l(u))\) and the \(C(l,u,\cdot)\)-successor is unique,
\[
\beta(\tau_l(u))=\tau_l(\beta(u)).
\]
Let \(F=\beta(\varnothing)\in U\). Since every \(u\in U\) is obtained from \(\varnothing\) by finitely many toggles,
\[
\beta(u)=u\triangle F.
\]

### The \(V\)-action is forced by \(T\)

Because \(P(l,x)\) is preserved and labels are fixed, \(\beta\) maps \(P_l\) to itself. The \(S(l)\)-structure on \(P_l\) is exactly two directed rays:
\[
R_{l,0},\quad R_{l,1}.
\]
The two roots \((l,0,0)\) and \((l,1,0)\) are the only elements of \(P_l\) with no \(S(l)\)-predecessor, so \(\beta\) either fixes the two rays or swaps them. Since each ray is a rigid directed \(\omega\)-ray, for some \(\epsilon_l\in\{0,1\}\),
\[
\beta(l,i,k)=(l,i\oplus\epsilon_l,k).
\]

Now use \(T\) with \(u=\varnothing\). Since
\[
T(l,\varnothing,(l,0,0))
\]
holds, applying \(\beta\) gives
\[
T(l,F,(l,\epsilon_l,0)).
\]
Thus \(\epsilon_l=\mathbf 1_{l\in F}\). Therefore
\[
\beta=\alpha_F.
\]

So
\[
\operatorname{Aut}(A)=\{\alpha_F:F\in[\omega]^{<\omega}\}\cong \bigoplus_{\omega} C_2.
\]
In particular, \(\operatorname{Aut}(A)\) is countable.

---

## (3) Every automorphism of every copy is computable relative to that copy

Let \(B\cong A\) be an arbitrary copy with atomic diagram oracle \(D(B)\), and let \(\rho\in\operatorname{Aut}(B)\).

By the automorphism classification, there is a finite set \(F_B\subseteq L^B\) of labels such that \(\rho\) toggles \(U^B\) by exactly the labels in \(F_B\), and swaps the two \(S(l)\)-rays exactly for \(l\in F_B\).

Because the computation is allowed to depend on \(B\) and \(\rho\), hardwire:
1. the finite list \(F_B\);
2. for each \(l\in F_B\), the two roots \(r^0_l,r^1_l\) of the two \(S(l)\)-rays.

Given input \(x\):

### If \(x\in L^B\)

Output \(x\).

### If \(x\in U^B\)

Starting with \(y=x\), for each \(l\in F_B\), search using \(D(B)\) for the unique \(z\) such that
\[
C^B(l,y,z).
\]
Replace \(y\) by \(z\). After finitely many toggles, output \(y\).

### If \(x\in V^B\)

Search for the unique label \(l\in L^B\) such that
\[
P^B(l,x).
\]
If \(l\notin F_B\), output \(x\).

If \(l\in F_B\), run two forward searches in parallel from the two hardwired roots \(r^0_l,r^1_l\). At stage \(n\), compute the \(n\)-th \(S(l)\)-successor of each root. If \(x\) appears at level \(n\) on one ray, output the level \(n\) element on the other ray.

This halts because every element of \(P_l^B\) lies at finite distance from exactly one of the two roots. The output is exactly \(\rho(x)\). Therefore
\[
\rho\leq_T D(B).
\]
Since \(B\) and \(\rho\) were arbitrary, the cone base is \(0\).

---

## (4) Parameter-restricted nondefinability

I interpret \(\Sigma^{\mathrm{in}}_1\) in the usual \(L_{\omega_1\omega}\) sense: countable disjunctions of existential formulas with finite existential tuples and quantifier-free infinitary matrices. The proof below is stronger than computable-infinitary nondefinability.

Let \(a\subseteq A\) be finite. Choose \(l\in L\) such that
\[
a\cap P_l=\varnothing.
\]
Let
\[
\pi=\alpha_{\{l\}}.
\]
Then
\[
p:=a\cup \pi(a)
\]
also satisfies
\[
p\cap P_l=\varnothing.
\]
Indeed, \(\pi\) sends \(U\) to \(U\), fixes labels, fixes every \(P_m\) for \(m\neq l\), and only moves elements of \(P_l\).

Let
\[
r_0=(l,0,0),\qquad r_1=(l,1,0),\qquad s_0=(l,0,1).
\]
Then
\[
\pi(r_0)=r_1,
\]
but
\[
\pi(s_0)=(l,1,1)\neq r_1.
\]

### Partial-shift lemma

Let \(D\) be any finite set containing \(p,r_0,r_1\) and some tuple of witnesses. Define a partial map \(\sigma:D\to A\) by
\[
\sigma(l,0,k)=(l,0,k+1)
\]
for elements of \(D\cap R_{l,0}\), and
\[
\sigma(x)=x
\]
for all other \(x\in D\).

Because \(p\cap P_l=\varnothing\), \(\sigma\) fixes every parameter in \(p\). Also,
\[
\sigma(r_0)=s_0,\qquad \sigma(r_1)=r_1.
\]

The map \(\sigma\) is an isomorphism from the finite induced substructure on \(D\) to the finite induced substructure on \(\sigma[D]\). Equality and sorts are preserved. The relations \(N\) and \(C\) are unaffected because they involve only \(L\) and \(U\), which are fixed. The relation \(P\) is preserved because shifted elements stay in \(P_l\). The relation \(S\) is preserved because
\[
S(l,(l,0,k),(l,0,m))
\iff m=k+1
\iff m+1=(k+1)+1
\iff S(l,(l,0,k+1),(l,0,m+1)).
\]
The relation \(T\) is preserved because shifted elements stay on side \(0\), and \(T(l,u,x)\) only sees the side of \(x\), not its level.

Thus \(\sigma\) preserves every atomic and negated atomic formula over \(p\). Hence it preserves every quantifier-free infinitary formula over \(p\).

### Consequence for existential formulas

Let
\[
\varphi(x,y;p)\equiv \exists \bar z\,\theta(x,y,\bar z;p)
\]
be an existential infinitary formula over \(p\), with \(\theta\) quantifier-free. Suppose
\[
A\models \varphi(r_0,r_1;p).
\]
Choose witnesses \(\bar c\). Let \(D\) contain \(p,r_0,r_1,\bar c\), and apply the partial shift above. Then
\[
A\models \theta(r_0,r_1,\bar c;p)
\]
iff
\[
A\models \theta(s_0,r_1,\sigma(\bar c);p).
\]
Therefore
\[
A\models \varphi(s_0,r_1;p).
\]

So every existential formula over \(p\) true at the genuine graph pair \((r_0,r_1)\) is also true at the false pair \((s_0,r_1)\).

Now suppose, toward contradiction, that \(\operatorname{Graph}(\pi)\) were \(\Sigma^{\mathrm{in}}_1\)-definable over \(p\). Write the defining formula as
\[
\bigvee_i \varphi_i(x,y;p),
\]
where each \(\varphi_i\) is existential. Since \((r_0,r_1)\in\operatorname{Graph}(\pi)\), some \(\varphi_i(r_0,r_1;p)\) holds. By the argument above, \(\varphi_i(s_0,r_1;p)\) holds. Hence the full disjunction holds at \((s_0,r_1)\), contradicting
\[
(s_0,r_1)\notin \operatorname{Graph}(\pi).
\]

Therefore \(\operatorname{Graph}(\pi)\) is not \(\Sigma^{\mathrm{in}}_1\)-definable over \(a\cup\pi(a)\).

Thus the proposed construction gives the desired positive example.

## Verdict: **valid after specified repairs/clarifications**

I do **not** see a concrete obstruction. The construction works for the intended diagonal claim, but the proof as stated needs tightening in three places: make the finite sorted language explicit, repair the arbitrary-copy computability algorithm so it does not wait forever on non-swapped gadgets, and replace “finite shift embedding” by a **strong partial isomorphism / strong self-embedding** argument to handle negated atomics.

### 1. Automorphisms: no hidden ones, assuming the intended sorted language

Use one \(P\)-sort, not infinitely many \(P_l\)-sorts. For example:

- \(L=\{\ell_0,\ell_1,\dots\}\) with directed successor on \(L\).
- \(U=[L]^{<\omega}\).
- \(P=\{(l,b,n):l\in L,\ b\in\{0,1\},\ n\in\omega\}\).
- \(S(l,(l,b,n),(l,b,n+1))\).
- \(T(l,u,(l,b,n))\) iff \(b=\mathbf 1_{l\in u}\).
- \(C(l,u,v)\) iff \(v=u\triangle\{l\}\).

With unary sort predicates, this is finite relational.

Now any automorphism \(g\):

1. Fixes \(L\) pointwise, since the one-way directed ray is rigid.
2. Acts on \(U\) by a finite translation. If \(F=g(\varnothing)\), then preservation of \(C\) gives  
   \[
   g(u\triangle\{l\})=g(u)\triangle\{l\},
   \]
   hence by connectedness of the finite-subset cube,
   \[
   g(u)=u\triangle F.
   \]
3. On each \(P_l\), \(T\) forces \(g\) to swap the two rays iff \(l\in F\). Then \(S\) forces preservation of level, since a one-way directed ray has no nontrivial automorphisms.

Conversely each finite-support \(\alpha_F\) plainly preserves \(C,S,T\). So
\[
\operatorname{Aut}(\mathcal A)=\{\alpha_F:F\subseteq_{\mathrm{fin}} L\}.
\]

### 2. \(T\) does not existentially define roots or level matching

It **does** let formulas identify sides, and if parameters include \(u,u\triangle\{l\}\), then \(l\) is existentially/atomically recoverable via \(C(l,u,u\triangle\{l\})\). That is harmless.

The key point is that \(T\) is level-blind. Even over arbitrary \(L\)- and \(U\)-parameters, the map shifting one chosen side of \(P_l\) forward by one step preserves all atomic and negated atomic facts among any finite tuple, provided no parameters lie in \(P_l\). Thus roots and same-level matching are not \(\Sigma^{\mathrm{in}}_1\)-definable from such parameters.

### 3. Arbitrary-copy AUT computability: repair the algorithm

The naive algorithm “search from the two hardwired roots and, if not found, output \(x\)” is not total, because for \(x\notin P_f\) the search never terminates.

Repair: in a copy \(\mathcal B\), for an automorphism with support \(F=\{f_0,\dots,f_{m-1}\}\), hardwire the labels \(f_i\) and the two roots of each swapped \(P_{f_i}\). Also either:

- add an incidence relation \(I(l,x)\) meaning \(x\in P_l\), or
- use \(T\) plus one \(U\)-element \(u_0\) to decide \(x\in P_{f_i}\): compute \(u_0\triangle\{f_i\}\) via \(C\), then
  \[
  x\in P_{f_i}
  \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
  T(f_i,u_0,x)\ \lor\ T(f_i,u_0\triangle\{f_i\},x).
  \]

Then the machine computes:

- If \(x\in U\), toggle by each hardwired \(f_i\) using \(C\).
- If \(x\in P_{f_i}\), search forward from the two hardwired roots until \(x\) appears, then output the same-level point on the other ray.
- If no \(i\) matches, output \(x\).

This is total relative to the complete atomic diagram of the copy. Hardwiring finitely many domain elements is legitimate for the usual nonuniform statement “each automorphism of each copy is computable relative to that copy.” It would not prove a stronger uniform version.

### 4. Non-\(\Sigma^{\mathrm{in}}_1\)-definability: the shift argument works if stated strongly

Fix finite parameters \(a\). Choose \(l\) such that \(a\cap P_l=\varnothing\). Then also \(\pi(a)\cap P_l=\varnothing\) for \(\pi=\alpha_{\{l\}}\).

Let \(r_0,r_1\) be the two roots of \(P_l\), and let \(s_0\) be the successor of \(r_0\) on side \(0\). Then
\[
\pi(r_0)=r_1,
\qquad
\pi(s_0)=s_1\neq r_1.
\]

Define a strong self-embedding \(e\) fixing everything except side \(0\) of \(P_l\), where it shifts
\[
(l,0,n)\mapsto(l,0,n+1).
\]
Then \(e\) fixes \(a\cup\pi(a)\), sends \(r_0\mapsto s_0\), and fixes \(r_1\).

Crucially, \(e\) is a strong embedding: it preserves and reflects atomic relations on its image. Thus it preserves quantifier-free formulas with negated atomics. Therefore every existential, and hence every \(\Sigma^{\mathrm{in}}_1\), formula true of \((r_0,r_1)\) over \(a\cup\pi(a)\) is also true of \((s_0,r_1)\).

So if the graph of \(\pi\) were \(\Sigma^{\mathrm{in}}_1\)-definable over \(a\cup\pi(a)\), then since \((r_0,r_1)\) is in the graph, \((s_0,r_1)\) would also be in the graph, contradiction.

This handles arbitrary witnesses and negated atomic subformulas.

### 5. Finite-language coding is fine

There is no infinite family of relation symbols. The label-indexed data are coded by arguments to \(C,S,T\). Multi-sorted language can be converted to one-sorted with finitely many unary predicates. A standard computable presentation is immediate by coding finite subsets of \(L\) and triples \((l,b,n)\).

### Important scope warning

Do not overclaim. A fixed \(\alpha_{\{l\}}\) may become \(\Sigma^{\mathrm{in}}_1\)-definable if the roots of \(P_l\) are allowed as parameters, because same-level matching can then be expressed by an infinitary disjunction over finite successor-chain lengths. The construction proves the stated **fresh-label diagonal** property: for every finite parameter set \(a\), choose a new \(l\) avoiding \(P_l\)-parameters.

# Problem We Are Solving
We consider infinite countable first-order structures, their automorphism groups and the descriptive aspects of their representations. A representation $\A$ of such a structure is an instance of its isomorphism type in which the universe or domain of the structure is the set of natural numbers $\N$ and the constants, functions and relations of the structure are elements of $\N$, multi-variable functions from $\N$ to $\N$ and multi-place relations on $\N$, respectively.   Similarly, an automorphism of $\A$ or an isomorphism from $\A$ to $\B$ is a bijective function from $\N$ to $\N$ that preserves the evaluation of all constants, functions and relations.  We let $\AUT(\A)$ denote the automorphism group of $\A$.

\begin{definition}\label{1.1}
  \begin{itemize}
  \item     $\A$ is \emph{computably $\AUT$-countable} if every automorphism of $\A$ is computable relative to $\A$.  
  \item  $\A$ is \emph{computably $\AUT$-countable on a cone} if there is a $C\subset\N$ such that whenever $\B$ is isomorphic to $\A$, every automorphism of $\B$ is computable relative to $\B\oplus C$.
  \end{itemize}
\end{definition}


\begin{definition}
  A function $f:\A\to\A$ is \emph{$\Sigma^{in}_1$-definable} in $\A$ relative to parameters
  $\bar{a}=(a_1,\dots,a_n)$ from $\A$ if there is a family of existential formulas $(\phi_i)_{i\in\N}$ such that for all $x$ and $y$ in $\A$, $f(x)=y$ if and only if there is an $i\in\N$ such that $\A\models\phi_i(\bar{a},x,y)$.
\end{definition}

\begin{question}\label{sec:question}\label{1.2}
  Is there a countable computably-represented structure $\A$ such that $\A$ is computably $\AUT$-countable on a cone and such that for any finite  set of parameters $\bar{a}$ in $\A$ there is an automorphism $\pi$ of $\A$ such that $\pi$ is not $\Sigma^{in}_1$-definable in $\A$ relative to the parameters $\bar{a}\cup \pi(\bar{a}),$ where $\pi(\bar{a})$ is the image of $\bar{a}$ under $\pi$.
\end{question}

Equip S∞ with its natural computable Polish presentation: if d is the usual ultrametric on ω^ω, set D(σ,τ) = (d(σ,τ)+d(σ^{-1},τ^{-1}))/2, and take the finite-support permutations as a computable dense set. For a subgroup G ≤ S∞, the following are equivalent: (i) G is effectively closed in the sense that there is a Π^0_1 subset P of Baire space ω^ω such that G = P ∩ S∞; (ii) G, as a subset of the computable Polish space (S∞,D), is effectively closed, i.e. the complement S∞ \ G is a computably enumerable union of D-basic open balls.  (from: literature_homepages_ecs_vuw_ac_nz_649d3b29c41f)
    Proof sketch: The key point is effective compatibility between the usual Baire-space metric d on ω^ω and the Polish-group metric D on S∞. Given a Baire basic open set U specified by a finite partial map σ, compute a D-open cover of U ∩ S∞. If σ is not injective then U ∩ S∞ is empty. If σ is injective, enumerate all finite injective extensions τ_i of σ^{-1} whose domains are finite initial segments of ω. The corresponding D-basic conditions, requiring a permutation to extend σ and its inverse to extend τ_i, cover exactly U ∩ S∞: every permutation extending σ has an inverse extending some τ_i, and conversely every permutation in such a D-basic set extends σ. Conversely, a D-basic open set is given by finite information about a permutation and its inverse; in the standard case this is a finite permutation σ of an initial segment, and the corresponding Baire cylinder U of all extensions of σ satisfies U ∩ S∞ equal to that D-basic set. These transformations are uniform and computable. Therefore an effective enumeration of basic open balls covering the complement in one presentation can be converted uniformly into such an enumeration in the other presentation, proving equivalence of the two Π^0_1 notions.

For finite tuples a,b ∈ ω^{<ω}, define a ∼_G b iff |a|=|b| and there is σ ∈ G such that σ(a)=b coordinatewise. Then the orbit equivalence relation ∼_G on ω^{<ω} is hyperarithmetic, uniformly in the tuple length.  (from: literature_homepages_ecs_vuw_ac_nz_649d3b29c41f)
    Proof sketch: By Proposition 1.3, an effectively closed subgroup of S∞ is a Π^0_2 subset of Baire space ω^ω, hence analytic. Since G is discrete as a subspace of the separable Polish group S∞, it has no nonempty perfect subset. The effective perfect set theorem for Σ^1_1 sets says that an analytic set with no perfect subset consists only of hyperarithmetic elements. Spector Σ^1_1 bounding then gives a single computable ordinal γ such that every σ ∈ G is computable from 0^(γ). Using the Π^0_2 definition of G and this uniform degree bound, 0^(γ+2) computes a listing of all elements of G: enumerate all 0^(γ)-computable total functions/permutations and test the Π^0_2 membership condition with two further jumps. Given such a listing (σ_i), for finite tuples a,b of the same length, a ∼_G b iff ∃i[σ_i(a)=b]. This is Σ^0_1 relative to the listing, and its negation is Π^0_1 relative to the listing; one more jump decides it. Thus ∼_G is computable from 0^(γ+3), uniformly for all finite tuple arities, hence hyperarithmetic.
  • [arXiv:homepages_ecs_vuw_ac_nz_649d3b29c41f Proposition 4.2] Let G ≤ S∞ be an effectively closed subgroup, and suppose its finite-tuple orbit equivalence relation ∼_G is hyperarithmetic. Duplicated orbit relations are harmless and avoid choosing representatives. Every σ ∈ G preserves all R_a, so G ≤ Aut(M). Conversely, if f ∈ Aut(M), then for every finite tuple a, M ⊨ R_a(f(a)), hence a ∼_G f(a), so some g_a ∈ G agrees with f on a. Thus every finite restriction of f is realized by an element of G; since G is closed in S∞, f ∈ G. Hence Aut(M)=G, and M is hyperarithmetic because ∼_G is. Next use the preservation lemma for hyperarithmetic structures: if M is Δ^0_γ for a computable ordinal γ, replace each relation value by a computable rigid linear-order gadget. The required uniform coding uses the Goncharov-Knight/Marker-style fact that membership in a Σ^0_γ (hence Δ^0_γ, by coding true and false cases) set can be uniformly represented by choosing between two rigid computable linear orders, e.g. suitable computable copies of ordinal orders such as ω^{γ+1} and ω^{γ+2}. The resulting expanded structure N is computable. Rigidity of the fibers implies every automorphism of N is determined by its restriction to the original M-sort, and the order type of each fiber forces that restriction to preserve exactly the relations of M. Conversely, every automorphism of M extends uniquely to N by the unique order isomorphisms between corresponding fibers. The restriction map Aut(N) → Aut(M) is therefore a group isomorphism. It is topological: finite constraints on base elements are preserved directly, while a finite constraint mentioning a fiber element q ∈ C^P_a maps, by rigidity, to a finite constraint on the image of the base tuple a, with either a uniquely determined compatible fiber image or no compatible automorphism. In the unrelativized effective theorem, if A is computable, R is computable, R is not relatively intrinsically computable, and a concrete effectiveness condition holds, then every c.e. degree is realized as the degree of R in some computable copy of A. Conversely, if R is formally d.c.e. — i.e. on the cone R is definable as a difference of two Σ^in_1-definable relations, equivalently by a d.c.e. infinitary definition — then the existence of such d-free tuples over every c is necessary for the spectrum on a cone to strictly contain the c.e. degrees. Equivalently, for formally d.c.e. R, if some tuple c has no d-free tuple over it, then in every copy on a sufficiently high cone the induced relation R^B has c.e. degree relative to the base oracle.  (from: literature_1412.3842)
    Proof sketch: The sufficiency direction is Proposition 3.9 relativized to a cone. In the computable version, assume effective access to d-free tuples and witnesses. Build a computable copy B by finite-injury partial isomorphisms Fs: initial segments of ω are mapped into A, and the diagram of B is the pullback limit. Requirements Re,i,j diagonalize against every alleged equivalence R^B = Φ_i^{W_e} and W_e = Φ_j^{R^B}, where W_e is c.e. A requirement chooses a fresh d-free tuple a over the current finite range and places it so that initially its R-pattern has one value. After computations R^B[≤n] = Φ_i^{W_e}[≤n] with use u and W_e[≤u] = Φ_j^{R^B}[≤u] with use v appear, d-freeness lets the construction replace a by a′ with the opposite relevant R-pattern while preserving the finite existential diagram, breaking the first computation. If W_e later changes below u, the second half of d-freeness supplies a′′ and witnesses restoring the old R-pattern below v while preserving all finite facts already committed; then the second computation is wrong because W_e changed but the R^B-oracle segment used by Φ_j was restored. Lower-priority strategies are injured; finite injury proves every requirement is met, so R^B is not of c.e. degree. Harizanov’s cone corollary then supplies all c.e. degrees as well, hence strict containment. The necessity direction is Proposition 3.5. If a tuple c has no d-free tuple and R is formally d.c.e., write R as α ∧ ¬β with α,β existential infinitary. In any copy B, define a c.e. set A of elements/tuples that appear to be in R at two stages and pass all finitely many non-d-freeness obstruction tests witnessed by formulas φ and ψ. One shows R^B ⊆ A, A − R^B is c.e. via β, and A is computable from R^B because a failed obstruction can be recognized using the actual R^B-pattern. Hence R^B ≡T A ⊕ (A − R^B), a join of c.e. sets, so R^B has c.e. degree. On a cone, the searches for the obstruction witnesses are computable, giving the stated relativized necessity.
  • [arXiv:math_berkeley_edu_34402455ae82 Lemma 3.2 (Type omitting lemma)] Let A be a structure in a countable vocabulary, let α be a countable ordinal, and let σ be a Π^in_{α+1} sentence such that A ⊨ σ. Let Φ(x) be a partial Π^in_α-type, i.e. a set of Π^in_α formulas in the finite tuple of variables x. Say that Φ is Σ^in_α-supported in A if there is a Σ^in_α formula θ(x) such that A ⊨ ∃x θ(x), and for every ψ(x) ∈ Φ, A ⊨ ∀x(θ(x) → ψ(x)). If Φ is not Σ^in_α-supported in A, then there is a countable structure B in the same vocabulary such that B ⊨ σ and B omits Φ, meaning that no tuple b from B satisfies every formula in Φ.  (from: literature_math_berkeley_edu_34402455ae82)
    Proof sketch: Put σ in normal form as a countable conjunction ∧_j ∀y_j σ_j(y_j), with each σ_j a Σ^in_α formula. Add fresh constants C = {c_0,c_1,...}. Build, by a Henkin construction, an increasing sequence of finite sets S_s of sentences in the expanded language, and assignments v_s of the constants mentioned in S_s into A, such that every sentence in S_s is true in A under v_s. Requirements scheduled over ω ensure: disjunctions in S have a chosen disjunct in S; existential formulas in S get witnesses among constants; conjunctions are decomposed; universals are instantiated at all constant tuples; every atomic sentence is decided consistently; for each j and tuple c, σ_j(c) is put into S; and for every tuple c of constants of the same length as x, some negation ¬ψ(c), ψ ∈ Φ, is put into S. The routine Henkin requirements preserve satisfiability in A by choosing the disjunct, witness, instance, or atomic truth that holds under the current assignment. The key type-omitting step is: given a tuple c and current finite S_s, let d be the other constants in S_s and let η(c,d) be the finite conjunction of S_s. Then ∃y η(x,y) is a Σ^in_α formula realized in A. Since Φ is not supported, this formula cannot imply all of Φ in A; hence for some ψ ∈ Φ there are a,b in A with A ⊨ η(a,b) ∧ ¬ψ(a). Add ¬ψ(c) and reset the assignment on c,d to a,b. Let S be the union. The complete consistent atomic part of S gives the Henkin/term model B, quotienting by equality if necessary. Induction on formula complexity using the decomposition and witness requirements shows every sentence in S holds in B. The σ_j(c) requirements yield B ⊨ σ, and the type-omitting requirements ensure each tuple of constants, hence each tuple of B, fails some formula from Φ.
  • [arXiv:math_berkeley_edu_34402455ae82 Theorem 1.1 and its continuation] Let A be a countable structure and let α < ω_1. For finite tuples u,v from A, write u ≤_γ v if every Π^in_γ formula true of u in A is true of v. A tuple a is α-free if for every β < α and every finite tuple d from A, there are tuples a′,d′ from A such that a d ≤_β a′ d′ but a is not ≤_α a′. A Π^in_α-type p(x) realized in A is Σ^in_α-supported within A if some Σ^in_α formula θ(x) is realized in A and implies, within A, every formula in p. Then the following are equivalent: (U1) for every finite tuple a from A, its automorphism orbit {b : some automorphism of A sends a to b} is definable in A without parameters by a Σ^in_α formula; (U2) A has a Π^in_{α+1} Scott sentence, i.e. a Π^in_{α+1} sentence whose countable models are exactly the copies of A; (U3) A is uniformly boldface Δ^0_α-categorical, i.e. after fixing a presentation of A there is a boldface Δ^0_α operator Θ such that for every presentation B of A, Θ(B) is an isomorphism from A onto B; (U4) the set of presentations of A, viewed as a subset of the standard Polish space of countable structures with domain ω in the given vocabulary, is Π^0_{α+1} in the Borel hierarchy; (U5) every Π^in_α-type realized in A is Σ^in_α-supported within A; (U6) there is a Π^in_{α+1} sentence χ true of A such that every structure B with B ⊨ χ satisfies B ≡_{α+1} A, equivalently B and A satisfy the same Σ^in_{α+1} sentences or are (α+1)-back-and-forth equivalent; (U7) no tuple in A is α-free.  (from: literature_math_berkeley_edu_34402455ae82)
    Proof sketch: The infinitary-logic core is U1⇒U2⇒U6⇒U5⇒U1. For U1⇒U2, let φ_a be a Σ^in_α definition of the orbit of each tuple a. The standard Scott-family sentence is the conjunction, over all tuples a, of ∀x[φ_a(x) → ((∧_{b∈A} ∃y φ_ab(x,y)) ∧ ∀y ∨_{b∈A} φ_ab(x,y))]. It says that every tuple in the orbit of a has exactly the possible one-point extensions appearing in A. Counting quantifier alternations gives a Π^in_{α+1} sentence. U2⇒U6 is immediate, taking the Scott sentence. For U6⇒U5, suppose p is a realized Π^in_α-type not Σ^in_α-supported. Lemma 3.2 applied to the Π^in_{α+1} sentence χ gives B ⊨ χ omitting p. By U6, B ≡_{α+1} A. Since B is countable, choose for each tuple of B a formula from p it fails; the resulting countable q ⊆ p is omitted in B. If a realizes p in A, then A ⊨ ∃x ∧_{ψ∈q} ψ(x), while B satisfies its negation. This is a Σ^in_{α+1} distinction, contradiction. For U5⇒U1, for each tuple a let φ_a support the Π^in_α-type of a. First φ_a(a) holds, since otherwise ¬φ_a would be in the type of a and would be implied by φ_a. Also, if A ⊨ φ_a(b), then A ⊨ φ_b(a); otherwise ¬φ_b is in the Π^in_α-type of a, so φ_a implies ¬φ_b, contradicting φ_a(b) and φ_b(b). Let P = {(a,b): A ⊨ φ_a(b)}. This relation has the back-and-forth property: if (a,b) ∈ P and d ∈ A, assume no c has φ_{b d}(a,c). Then ∀y ¬φ_{b d}(a,y) belongs to the Π^in_α-type of a, so φ_a implies it; applying φ_a(b) gives ∀y ¬φ_{b d}(b,y), contradicting φ_{b d}(b,d). Thus choose c with φ_{b d}(a,c), and symmetry gives φ_{a c}(b,d). Back-and-forth yields an automorphism sending a to b, so φ_a defines the orbit. The remaining equivalences are imported standard facts: U2⇔U4 is López-Escobar's theorem; U1⇔U3 is the Ash/Ash-Knight-Manasse-Slaman characterization of uniform boldface Δ^0_α categoricity by Σ^in_α Scott families; U1⇔U7 is Ash-Knight's α-freeness characterization, relativized to a sufficiently large oracle and in the parameter-free version.
  • [arXiv:math_berkeley_edu_34402455ae82 Theorem 2.5 (including Theorem 2.4 as α = 1)] Let A be a countable structure and let α be a countable ordinal. The following are equivalent: (C1) A is Δ^0_α-categorical on a cone, meaning that there is a real Y such that for every X ≥_T Y, whenever B and C are X-computable presentations of A, there is an isomorphism B ≅ C of complexity Δ^0_α relative to X; (C2) A has a Σ^in_{α+2} Scott sentence; (C3) there is a finite tuple a from A such that the expansion (A,a) by constants naming a has categoricity Scott rank at most α, equivalently every automorphism orbit of a finite tuple in (A,a) is definable without parameters in the expanded language by a Σ^in_α formula. In particular, taking α = 1, A is computably categorical on a cone if and only if A has a Σ^in_3 Scott sentence.  (from: literature_math_berkeley_edu_34402455ae82)
    Proof sketch: The proof combines the uniform categoricity facts with Theorem 1.1 and the usual parameter trick. Ventsov's theorem, together with Ash's higher-level version, says that relative Δ^0_α-categoricity becomes uniform Δ^0_α-categoricity after naming finitely many parameters; on a cone all non-uniform choices, codes for operators, and presentations can be absorbed into the base oracle. Thus C1 is equivalent to the existence of a finite tuple a such that (A,a) is uniformly boldface Δ^0_α-categorical. By Theorem 1.1, this is equivalent to every tuple orbit in (A,a) being Σ^in_α-definable, i.e. C3, and also equivalent to (A,a) having a Π^in_{α+1} Scott sentence. If (A,a) has such a Π^in_{α+1} Scott sentence θ with constants for a, replace those constants by variables and existentially quantify them: ∃x θ(x) is a Σ^in_{α+2} Scott sentence for the reduct A, giving C2. Conversely, a Σ^in_{α+2} Scott sentence for A can be put into normal form as a countable disjunction of existential tuples followed by Π^in_{α+1} formulas. Choosing a disjunct and witnesses realized in A yields finite parameters a; the countable categoricity of the original sentence, together with the same Ventsov/Ash parameter argument, gives a Π^in_{α+1} Scott sentence and hence uniform boldface Δ^0_α-categoricity for (A,a). Applying Theorem 1.1 gives C3. For α = 1, Δ^0_1 relative to X is X-computability, so C1 is precisely computable categoricity on a cone and C2 is the Σ^in_3 Scott-sentence condition of Theorem 2.4.
  • [arXiv:1506.02737 AKMS–Chisholm intrinsic definability characterization, quoted after Definition 1] Let A be a countable structure in a computable language, and let R⊆A^{<ω}. Say that R is uniformly relatively intrinsically computably enumerable, u.r.i.c.e., if there is a c.e. operator W such that for every copy (B,R^B) of (A,R), where B has domain ω and D(B) is its atomic diagram, R^B = W^{D(B)}. Say that R is uniformly relatively intrinsically computable, u.r.i. computable, if there is a computable operator Ψ such that for every copy (B,R^B) of (A,R), R^B = Ψ^{D(B)}. Then R is u.r.i.c.e. if and only if R is definable in A, without parameters, by computably infinitary Σ^c_1 formulas: for R⊆A^{<ω}, this means that there is a computable list φ_1,φ_2,φ_3,... of Σ^c_1 formulas such that φ_n defines R∩A^n. Moreover, R is u.r.i. computable if and only if both R and its complement are definable without parameters by computably infinitary Σ^c_1 formulas, equivalently R is Δ^c_1-definable. The same convention applies uniformly to a sequence of relations (R_i : i∈ω), R_i⊆A^{<ω}: the sequence is u.r.i.c.e. or Σ^c_1-definable when the associated subset of A^{<ω}×ω, using an effective coding of A^{<ω}×ω into A^{<ω}, has that property.  (from: literature_1506.02737)
    Proof sketch: The paper cites Ash–Knight–Manasse–Slaman and Chisholm for the finite-arity theorem and Mon12 for the extension to A^{<ω}. The easy direction is that a computable infinitary existential definition can be enumerated from the atomic diagram of any copy: enumerate the computable disjunctions and all finite witness tuples, and use D(B) to check the quantifier-free atomic diagram requirements. If both R and its complement have such enumerations, run them in parallel to compute the characteristic function. Conversely, from a uniform c.e. operator W, build a Σ^c_1 definition by listing every finite partial atomic diagram δ, with distinguished free tuple positions, such that W with oracle δ already enumerates the corresponding tuple. Convert δ into a finitary existential formula saying that the free tuple, together with some extra variables, realizes δ. The disjunction over all such finite successful computations is computable. Correctness uses intrinsicity: if a tuple in A satisfies one of these finite diagrams, extend that finite configuration to a copy B of A; the same finite oracle computation makes W enumerate the tuple, so it is in R. Conversely, if a tuple is in R, put it into the distinguished positions in some copy; W enumerates it after finite use, producing one of the disjuncts. The A^{<ω} and sequence versions are obtained by the paper’s effective coding of variable-length tuples and the extra index i into tuples from A.
  • [arXiv:1506.02737 Theorem 5 with the Proposition 14 construction] Let A=(A;P_0^A,P_1^A,...) and B be countable structures in computable relational languages, with P_i of computable arity p(i). Let Iso(A) and Iso(B) be the categories whose objects are copies of A and B with domain ω and whose morphisms are isomorphisms. A computable functor F:Iso(B)→Iso(A) is a functor together with Turing operators Φ and Φ_* such that, for every copy B̃ of B, Φ^{D(B̃)} is the atomic diagram of F(B̃), and for every isomorphism h:B̃→B̂, Φ_*^{D(B̃)⊕h⊕D(B̂)} is the isomorphism F(h):F(B̃)→F(B̂). An effective interpretation of A in B consists of parameter-free Δ^c_1-definable relations Dom_B^A⊆B^{<ω}, an equivalence relation ∼ on Dom_B^A, and relations R_i⊆(B^{<ω})^{p(i)} closed under ∼, uniformly in i, such that (Dom_B^A/∼;R_0/∼,R_1/∼,...) is isomorphic to A. Then A is effectively interpretable in B if and only if there exists a computable functor Iso(B)→Iso(A). In the nontrivial direction, given F=(Φ,Φ_*), the interpretation may be chosen explicitly as follows, after coding B^{<ω}×ω into B^{<ω} and after identifying A with the copy F(B_0) for a fixed presentation B_0 of B, or composing with any fixed isomorphism F(B_0)≅A. For a tuple b̄∈B^n, let D(b̄) be the finite atomic diagram of the first n elements in the pullback copy determined by any bijection f:ω→B extending b̄, restricted as in the paper to the first n relation symbols, and let λ be the identity map on ω. Put (b̄,i) in Dom_B^A iff Φ_*^{D(b̄)⊕(λ↾n)⊕D(b̄)}(i) converges with value i. For (b̄,i),(c̄,j)∈Dom_B^A, let c̄′ list the elements of c̄ not occurring in b̄ and b̄′ list the elements of b̄ not occurring in c̄. Declare (b̄,i)∼(c̄,j) iff there is a finite tuple d̄ of elements not occurring in b̄ or c̄ and a finite permutation σ with (b̄ c̄′ d̄)=(c̄ b̄′ d̄)σ such that Φ_*^{D(b̄ c̄′ d̄)⊕σ⊕D(c̄ b̄′ d̄)}(i)=j and Φ_*^{D(c̄ b̄′ d̄)⊕σ^{-1}⊕D(b̄ c̄′ d̄)}(j)=i. Finally, for P_k of arity p, put ((b̄_1,i_1),...,(b̄_p,i_p)) in R_k iff there are a tuple c̄ and numbers j_1,...,j_p such that (b̄_s,i_s)∼(c̄,j_s) for all s≤p and the finite output Φ^{D(c̄)} already asserts the atomic fact P_k(j_1,...,j_p). These Dom_B^A, ∼, and R_k are uniformly Δ^c_1-definable without parameters and yield an effective interpretation of A in B.  (from: literature_1506.02737)
    Proof sketch: Interpretation to functor is direct. From D(B̃), the Δ^c_1 definitions compute the interpreted domain, equivalence relation, and relations. Choose a canonical D(B̃)-computable bijection τ^{B̃}:ω→Dom_{B̃}^A/∼ and pull the interpreted relations back to ω to form F(B̃). For an isomorphism h:B̃→B̂, extend h to tuples and set F(h)=(τ^{B̂})^{-1}∘h∘τ^{B̃}; this is computable and functorial. Conversely, given Φ,Φ_*, a pair (b̄,i) is intended to represent the i-th element of the copy Φ^{D(B^f)} whenever f extends b̄; the identity condition in Dom forces i to be stable under changing the unused part of f. The relation ∼ says that finite information already makes Φ_* send i to j between two such pullback copies. Dom and its complement are Σ^c_1 because convergence, wrong output, and finite-oracle divergence are detected from finite diagrams. For ∼, define a Σ^c_1 relation ≁ by the same kind of finite witness but with output not equal to the required value. Totality of Φ_* on a genuine total isomorphism extending the finite permutation shows that either ∼ or ≁ holds. They cannot both hold: extend the two finite witnesses to bijections f_1,g_1,f_2,g_2 onto B; the resulting square of copies gives a factorization of one isomorphism through the others, and functoriality F(u∘v)=F(u)∘F(v), together with the Dom identity computations, would force the same value and the different value simultaneously. Thus ∼ and its complement are Σ^c_1. Symmetry is built in, reflexivity comes from Dom, and transitivity is proved by composing finite witnesses through suitable pullback copies and applying functoriality. The definitions of R_k and of its complement Q_k, obtained by requiring ¬P_k, are Σ^c_1. They are complements because any tuple can be moved to a common initial-segment representative, and if both P_k and ¬P_k were witnessed, the isomorphism between the corresponding functor outputs would both preserve and fail to preserve P_k. Finally, every class has an initial-segment representative, and if b̄⊆c̄ then (b̄,i)∼(c̄,j) iff i=j. Hence i↦(B_0↾n,i), for the least n with (B_0↾n,i)∈Dom, induces a bijective relation-preserving map from F(B_0) onto the quotient.
  • [arXiv:1506.02737 Proposition 7] Let F:Iso(B)→Iso(A) be a computable functor. Apply the construction of Proposition 14 to F to obtain an effective interpretation of A in B, and then apply the construction of Proposition 13 to that interpretation to obtain a computable functor I_F:Iso(B)→Iso(A). Then F and I_F are effectively naturally isomorphic. That is, there exists a computable Turing functional Λ such that for every copy B̃ of B, Λ^{D(B̃)} is an isomorphism Λ_{B̃}:F(B̃)→I_F(B̃), and for every isomorphism h:B̃→B̂ the naturality equation I_F(h)∘Λ_{B̃}=Λ_{B̂}∘F(h) holds.  (from: literature_1506.02737)
    Proof sketch: For each copy B̃, Proposition 14 and Lemma 22 give a uniformly D(B̃)-computable isomorphism F_{B̃}:F(B̃)→Dom_{B̃}^A/∼, sending an element i of F(B̃) to its canonical least initial-segment representative in the interpreted quotient. Proposition 13, applied to this interpretation, constructs I_F(B̃) by choosing a uniformly computable bijection τ^{B̃}:ω→Dom_{B̃}^A/∼ and pulling the interpreted structure back along τ^{B̃}; thus τ^{B̃} is an isomorphism I_F(B̃)→Dom_{B̃}^A/∼. Define Λ_{B̃}=(τ^{B̃})^{-1}∘F_{B̃}. This is uniformly computable from D(B̃). To prove naturality, let h:B̃→B̂ be an isomorphism and take i∈F(B̃), with j=F(h)(i). Suppose F_{B̃}(i)=(ā,i) and F_{B̂}(j)=(b̄,j) after possibly using longer initial segments. Since Φ_*^{D(B̃)⊕h⊕D(B̂)}(i)=j, enlarge ā and b̄ until this computation is witnessed by finite oracles; then the definition of ∼ in B̂ gives h(ā,i)∼(b̄,j). Hence the square involving F(h), h on interpreted domains, and the maps F_{B̃},F_{B̂} commutes modulo ∼. The square involving h, τ^{B̃},τ^{B̂}, and I_F(h) commutes by the definition of I_F(h). Combining the two squares gives I_F(h)∘Λ_{B̃}=Λ_{B̂}∘F(h).
  • [arXiv:math_berkeley_edu_2c8e4339dec3 Lemma 6.2] Let A be a computable countable structure. Say that A is effectively ∃-atomic if it has a computably enumerable Scott family consisting of existential first-order formulas: a c.e. set S of existential formulas such that every finite tuple from A satisfies some formula in S, and any two tuples satisfying the same formula from S are in the same automorphism orbit of A. Say that A is uniformly effectively 1-prime if there is a Turing functional Φ such that, for every computable model B of the ∀2-theory of A, Φ with oracle the atomic diagram D(B) computes a 1-embedding from A into B, where a 1-embedding is an embedding preserving universal formulas on tuples. Then A is effectively ∃-atomic if and only if A is uniformly effectively 1-prime.  (from: literature_math_berkeley_edu_2c8e4339dec3)
    Proof sketch: Forward direction: from a c.e. existential Scott family, uniformly build a 1-embedding f:A→B for any computable B satisfying the ∀2-theory of A. Enumerate A as a0,a1,.... At stage s, find an existential Scott-family formula φs satisfied by (a0,...,as). Given f(a0),...,f(as−1) already chosen satisfying φs−1, use that A satisfies the ∀2 sentence ∀x0...xs−1(φs−1(x0,...,xs−1) → ∃xs φs(x0,...,xs)); hence B satisfies it, so a witness for f(as) exists and can be found by search relative to D(B). If θ is universal and A⊨θ(a0,...,as), then A⊨∀x(φs(x)→θ(x)), another ∀2 fact, so B⊨θ(f(a0),...,f(as)); thus f is a 1-embedding. Reverse direction: assume Φ is uniform. The key finite-use/permutation extraction is as follows. Fix a finite tuple u from A and run Φ^{D(A)}(u), obtaining an output tuple v; since the output is a 1-embedding A→A, u and v have the same universal type. Choose s large enough that this computation has halted, uses only D(A)↾s, and the finitely many natural numbers occurring in v are among the first s positions. Form an existential formula θu(x) saying that x can be placed in the coordinate positions occupied by v inside a tuple of length s whose finite atomic diagram is exactly D(A)↾s; formally, existentially quantify the other coordinates and assert the corresponding finite atomic diagram. If a tuple b in A satisfies θu, with witnesses d for the other coordinates, make a new presentation Ã of A by a finite permutation so that the first s presented elements are arranged as in the witnessing tuple, with b in the positions corresponding to v. Then D(Ã)↾s=D(A)↾s, so the same finite computation forces Φ^{D(Ã)}(u) to output the same coordinate tuple, which now denotes b. Because Φ^{D(Ã)} is a 1-embedding, b has the same universal type as u. Hence θu supports the universal type of u. The paper phrases the same maneuver by choosing a preimage of a target initial segment under Φ^{D(A)} and then using the formula ∃y φ^at_{D(A)↾s}(x,y); the permutation argument is identical. Since A is computable and Φ is fixed, these supporting existential formulas are obtained uniformly effectively for all tuples. By the back-and-forth argument proving Theorem 1.2, uniformly supporting all realized universal types yields a c.e. existential Scott family.
  • [arXiv:math_berkeley_edu_2c8e4339dec3 Theorem 1.2] Let A be a countable structure. For a tuple a from A, let orb_A(a) be its orbit under Aut(A). A is ∃-atomic if for every finite tuple a there is an existential formula φ_a(x) whose realizations in A are exactly orb_A(a). A type p(x) realized in A is ∃-supported in A if some existential formula φ(x), realized in A, implies every formula in p within A. A 1-embedding is an embedding preserving universal formulas on tuples, and A is 1-prime if every countable model of the ∀2-theory of A admits a 1-embedding of A into it. Then the following are equivalent: (A1) A is ∃-atomic. (A2) A has an infinitary Π2 Scott sentence. (A3) A is uniformly continuously categorical, i.e. there is a continuous operator which, from the atomic diagram of any copy B of A, outputs an isomorphism from B to A. (A4) Every first-order type realized in A is ∃-supported in A. (A5) Every universal type realized in A is ∃-supported in A. (A6) A is 1-prime.  (from: literature_math_berkeley_edu_2c8e4339dec3)
    Proof sketch: A1⇒A4⇒A5 is immediate: an existential formula defining the automorphism orbit of a tuple implies every first-order, hence every universal, formula true of that tuple. The central support-to-orbit step is A5⇒A1. For each tuple a choose an existential φ_a supporting ∀-tp_A(a). First φ_a(a) must hold; otherwise ¬φ_a, a universal formula, belongs to ∀-tp_A(a), contradicting that φ_a is realized and implies the type. Define I={(a,b):A⊨φ_a(b)}. If A⊨φ_a(b), then symmetry holds: if not A⊨φ_b(a), then ¬φ_b∈∀-tp_A(a), so φ_a would imply ¬φ_b, contradicted by b. Thus I is symmetric. I has back-and-forth: atomic agreement follows from agreement of universal/quantifier-free facts; given (a,b)∈I and c∈A, if no d has (ac,bd)∈I, then ¬∃y φ_ac(b,y) is universal true of b, hence, using symmetry and support, true of a, contradicting witness c. The back condition follows symmetrically. A standard back-and-forth construction gives an automorphism taking a to any b satisfying φ_a, so φ_a defines orb_A(a). A1⇒A2 is Scott's construction specialized to existential orbit formulas: a Π2 sentence says that for every tuple satisfying its orbit formula, the induced relation I_B={(a,b):B⊨φ_a(b)} has atomic agreement plus forth and back extensions. In any B satisfying the ∀2-theory of A, recursively choose images satisfying φ_s; existence of the next image is guaranteed by the ∀2 sentence ∀x(φ_s→∃y φ_{s+1}), and preservation of any universal θ true in A follows from the ∀2 sentence ∀x(φ_s→θ). A6⇒A1: if some realized universal type were not ∃-supported, omit it in a model B of the ∀2-theory of A; then no 1-embedding A→B could exist. Yes—the candidate works. I see no structural flaw. The only small clarifications I would make are: declare all relations false outside their intended sorts, and in the copy-computation algorithm search from **both** hardwired roots of a toggled \(S(l)\)-gadget, not just one.

## Construction

Use the finite relational language
\[
\{L,U,V,N,C,P,S,T\},
\]
where \(L,U,V\) are unary, \(N,P\) are binary, and \(C,S,T\) are ternary.

Let
\[
L=\omega,\qquad U=[\omega]^{<\omega},\qquad V=\omega\times 2\times \omega.
\]
Write \(P_l=\{(l,i,k):i<2,\ k\in\omega\}\), and let
\[
R_{l,i}=\{(l,i,k):k\in\omega\}.
\]
Define:
- \(N(l,m)\iff m=l+1\).
- \(C(l,u,v)\iff v=u\triangle\{l\}\).
- \(P(l,x)\iff x=(l,i,k)\) for some \(i,k\).
- \(S(l,(l,i,k),(l,i,k+1))\) for \(i<2,k\in\omega\).
- \(T(l,u,(l,i,k))\iff i=\mathbf 1_{l\in u}\).

All relations are false on wrongly sorted tuples.

For finite \(F\subseteq \omega\), define
\[
\alpha_F(l)=l,\qquad
\alpha_F(u)=u\triangle F,\qquad
\alpha_F(l,i,k)=(l,i\oplus \mathbf 1_{l\in F},k).
\]

---

## (1) Computability

Use a standard computable tagged coding:
- \(L\)-elements are \(\langle 0,n\rangle\).
- \(U\)-elements are \(\langle 1,e\rangle\), where \(e\) codes the finite set whose characteristic function is the binary expansion of \(e\).
- \(V\)-elements are \(\langle 2,l,i,k\rangle\), with \(i<2\).

Then all sort predicates are decidable. Also:
\[
C(l,e,e')\iff e'= e\oplus 2^l,
\]
and
\[
T(l,e,(l,i,k))\iff i=\text{the }l\text{th bit of }e.
\]
The relations \(N,P,S\) are plainly decidable from the tuple coordinates. Hence this is a computable presentation of \(A\).

---

## (2) Automorphism group

First, every \(\alpha_F\) is an automorphism. The label sort \(L\) and \(N\) are fixed. For \(C\),
\[
(u\triangle F)\triangle\{l\}=(u\triangle\{l\})\triangle F.
\]
For \(S\), the level \(k\) is preserved and, for each fixed \(l\), either both rays are fixed or both are swapped. For \(T\),
\[
\mathbf 1_{l\in u\triangle F}
=
\mathbf 1_{l\in u}\oplus \mathbf 1_{l\in F},
\]
exactly matching the side flip in \(V\).

Conversely, let \(\beta\in\operatorname{Aut}(A)\).

### Labels are fixed

Inside \(L\), the element \(0\) is the unique \(L\)-element with no \(N\)-predecessor. Hence \(\beta(0)=0\). If \(\beta(n)=n\), then \(\beta(n+1)\) is the unique \(N\)-successor of \(n\), so \(\beta(n+1)=n+1\). Thus \(\beta\) fixes \(L\) pointwise.

### The \(U\)-action is translation by a finite set

For each \(l\), let \(\tau_l(u)=u\triangle\{l\}\). Since \(C(l,u,\tau_l(u))\) and the \(C(l,u,\cdot)\)-successor is unique,
\[
\beta(\tau_l(u))=\tau_l(\beta(u)).
\]
Let \(F=\beta(\varnothing)\in U\). Since every \(u\in U\) is obtained from \(\varnothing\) by finitely many toggles,
\[
\beta(u)=u\triangle F.
\]

### The \(V\)-action is forced by \(T\)

Because \(P(l,x)\) is preserved and labels are fixed, \(\beta\) maps \(P_l\) to itself. The \(S(l)\)-structure on \(P_l\) is exactly two directed rays:
\[
R_{l,0},\quad R_{l,1}.
\]
The two roots \((l,0,0)\) and \((l,1,0)\) are the only elements of \(P_l\) with no \(S(l)\)-predecessor, so \(\beta\) either fixes the two rays or swaps them. Since each ray is a rigid directed \(\omega\)-ray, for some \(\epsilon_l\in\{0,1\}\),
\[
\beta(l,i,k)=(l,i\oplus\epsilon_l,k).
\]

Now use \(T\) with \(u=\varnothing\). Since
\[
T(l,\varnothing,(l,0,0))
\]
holds, applying \(\beta\) gives
\[
T(l,F,(l,\epsilon_l,0)).
\]
Thus \(\epsilon_l=\mathbf 1_{l\in F}\). Therefore
\[
\beta=\alpha_F.
\]

So
\[
\operatorname{Aut}(A)=\{\alpha_F:F\in[\omega]^{<\omega}\}\cong \bigoplus_{\omega} C_2.
\]
In particular, \(\operatorname{Aut}(A)\) is countable.

---

## (3) Every automorphism of every copy is computable relative to that copy

Let \(B\cong A\) be an arbitrary copy with atomic diagram oracle \(D(B)\), and let \(\rho\in\operatorname{Aut}(B)\).

By the automorphism classification, there is a finite set \(F_B\subseteq L^B\) of labels such that \(\rho\) toggles \(U^B\) by exactly the labels in \(F_B\), and swaps the two \(S(l)\)-rays exactly for \(l\in F_B\).

Because the computation is allowed to depend on \(B\) and \(\rho\), hardwire:
1. the finite list \(F_B\);
2. for each \(l\in F_B\), the two roots \(r^0_l,r^1_l\) of the two \(S(l)\)-rays.

Given input \(x\):

### If \(x\in L^B\)

Output \(x\).

### If \(x\in U^B\)

Starting with \(y=x\), for each \(l\in F_B\), search using \(D(B)\) for the unique \(z\) such that
\[
C^B(l,y,z).
\]
Replace \(y\) by \(z\). After finitely many toggles, output \(y\).

### If \(x\in V^B\)

Search for the unique label \(l\in L^B\) such that
\[
P^B(l,x).
\]
If \(l\notin F_B\), output \(x\).

If \(l\in F_B\), run two forward searches in parallel from the two hardwired roots \(r^0_l,r^1_l\). At stage \(n\), compute the \(n\)-th \(S(l)\)-successor of each root. If \(x\) appears at level \(n\) on one ray, output the level \(n\) element on the other ray.

This halts because every element of \(P_l^B\) lies at finite distance from exactly one of the two roots. The output is exactly \(\rho(x)\). Therefore
\[
\rho\leq_T D(B).
\]
Since \(B\) and \(\rho\) were arbitrary, the cone base is \(0\).

## (4) Parameter-restricted nondefinability

I interpret \(\Sigma^{\mathrm{in}}_1\) in the usual \(L_{\omega_1\omega}\) sense: countable disjunctions of existential formulas with finite existential tuples and quantifier-free infinitary matrices. The proof below is stronger than computable-infinitary nondefinability.

Let \(a\subseteq A\) be finite. Choose \(l\in L\) such that
\[
a\cap P_l=\varnothing.
\]
Let
\[
\pi=\alpha_{\{l\}}.
\]
Then
\[
p:=a\cup \pi(a)
\]
also satisfies
\[
p\cap P_l=\varnothing.
\]
Indeed, \(\pi\) sends \(U\) to \(U\), fixes labels, fixes every \(P_m\) for \(m\neq l\), and only moves elements of \(P_l\).

Let
\[
r_0=(l,0,0),\qquad r_1=(l,1,0),\qquad s_0=(l,0,1).
\]
Then
\[
\pi(r_0)=r_1,
\]
but
\[
\pi(s_0)=(l,1,1)\neq r_1.
\]

### Partial-shift lemma

Let \(D\) be any finite set containing \(p,r_0,r_1\) and some tuple of witnesses. Define a partial map \(\sigma:D\to A\) by
\[
\sigma(l,0,k)=(l,0,k+1)
\]
for elements of \(D\cap R_{l,0}\), and
\[
\sigma(x)=x
\]
for all other \(x\in D\).

Because \(p\cap P_l=\varnothing\), \(\sigma\) fixes every parameter in \(p\). Also,
\[
\sigma(r_0)=s_0,\qquad \sigma(r_1)=r_1.
\]

The map \(\sigma\) is an isomorphism from the finite induced substructure on \(D\) to the finite induced substructure on \(\sigma[D]\). Equality and sorts are preserved. The relations \(N\) and \(C\) are unaffected because they involve only \(L\) and \(U\), which are fixed. The relation \(P\) is preserved because shifted elements stay in \(P_l\). The relation \(S\) is preserved because
\[
S(l,(l,0,k),(l,0,m))
\iff m=k+1
\iff m+1=(k+1)+1
\iff S(l,(l,0,k+1),(l,0,m+1)).
\]
The relation \(T\) is preserved because shifted elements stay on side \(0\), and \(T(l,u,x)\) only sees the side of \(x\), not its level.

Thus \(\sigma\) preserves every atomic and negated atomic formula over \(p\). Hence it preserves every quantifier-free infinitary formula over \(p\).

### Consequence for existential formulas

Let
\[
\varphi(x,y;p)\equiv \exists \bar z\,\theta(x,y,\bar z;p)
\]
be an existential infinitary formula over \(p\), with \(\theta\) quantifier-free. Suppose
\[
A\models \varphi(r_0,r_1;p).
\]
Choose witnesses \(\bar c\). Let \(D\) contain \(p,r_0,r_1,\bar c\), and apply the partial shift above. Then
\[
A\models \theta(r_0,r_1,\bar c;p)
\]
iff
\[
A\models \theta(s_0,r_1,\sigma(\bar c);p).
\]
Therefore
\[
A\models \varphi(s_0,r_1;p).
\]

So every existential formula over \(p\) true at the genuine graph pair \((r_0,r_1)\) is also true at the false pair \((s_0,r_1)\).

Now suppose, toward contradiction, that \(\operatorname{Graph}(\pi)\) were \(\Sigma^{\mathrm{in}}_1\)-definable over \(p\). Write the defining formula as
\[
\bigvee_i \varphi_i(x,y;p),
\]
where each \(\varphi_i\) is existential. Since \((r_0,r_1)\in\operatorname{Graph}(\pi)\), some \(\varphi_i(r_0,r_1;p)\) holds. By the argument above, \(\varphi_i(s_0,r_1;p)\) holds. Hence the full disjunction holds at \((s_0,r_1)\), contradicting
\[
(s_0,r_1)\notin \operatorname{Graph}(\pi).
\]

Therefore \(\operatorname{Graph}(\pi)\) is not \(\Sigma^{\mathrm{in}}_1\)-definable over \(a\cup\pi(a)\).

Thus the proposed construction gives the desired positive example.
