# Solution
## Original Problem

We consider infinite countable first-order structures, their automorphism groups and the descriptive aspects of their representations. A representation $\A$ of such a structure is an instance of its isomorphism type in which the universe or domain of the structure is the set of natural numbers $\N$ and the constants, functions and relations of the structure are elements of $\N$, multi-variable functions from $\N$ to $\N$ and multi-place relations on $\N$, respectively.   Similarly, an automorphism of $\A$ or an isomorphism from $\A$ to $\B$ is a bijective function from $\N$ to $\N$ that preserves the evaluation of all constants, functions and relations.  We let $\AUT(\A)$ denote the automorphism group of $\A$.

\begin{definition}\label{1.1}
  \begin{itemize}
  \item     $\A$ is \emph{computably $\AUT$-countable} if every automorphism of $\A$ is computable relative to $\A$.  
  \item  $\A$ is \emph{computably $\AUT$-countable on a cone} if there is a $C\subset\N$ such that whenever $\B$ is isomorphic to $\A$, every automorphism of $\B$ is computable relative to $\B\oplus C$.
  \end{itemize}
\end{definition}


\begin{definition}
  A function $f:\A\to\A$ is \emph{$\Sigma^{in}_1$-definable} in $\A$ relative to parameters
  $\bar{a}=(a_1,\dots,a_n)$ from $\A$ if there is a family of existential formulas $(\phi_i)_{i\in\N}$ such that for all $x$ and $y$ in $\A$, $f(x)=y$ if and only if there is an $i\in\N$ such that $\A\models\phi_i(\bar{a},x,y)$.
\end{definition}

\begin{question}\label{sec:question}\label{1.2}
  Is there a countable computably-represented structure $\A$ such that $\A$ is computably $\AUT$-countable on a cone and such that for any finite  set of parameters $\bar{a}$ in $\A$ there is an automorphism $\pi$ of $\A$ such that $\pi$ is not $\Sigma^{in}_1$-definable in $\A$ relative to the parameters $\bar{a}\cup \pi(\bar{a}),$ where $\pi(\bar{a})$ is the image of $\bar{a}$ under $\pi$.
\end{question}

## Problem Solved

The proof establishes the following exact positive answer.

There is a computable presentation on domain \(\omega=\mathbb N\) of a countable one-sorted first-order structure \(\mathcal A\) in the finite relational language
\[
\{L,U,V,N,I,C,S,T\},
\]
where \(L,U,V\) are unary predicates, \(N\) and \(I\) are binary predicates, and \(C,S,T\) are ternary predicates. Abstractly, \(\mathcal A\) has three named unary sorts:
\[
L^{\mathcal A}=\{\ell_n:n\in\omega\},\qquad
U^{\mathcal A}=[\omega]^{<\omega},\qquad
V^{\mathcal A}=\{(n,b,k):n,k\in\omega,\ b\in\{0,1\}\}.
\]
The relations are:
\[
N(\ell_n,\ell_{n+1});
\]
\[
C(\ell_n,F,G)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad G=F\triangle\{n\};
\]
\[
I(\ell_n,(m,b,k))\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad m=n;
\]
\[
S(\ell_n,(n,b,k),(n,b,k+1));
\]
and
\[
T(\ell_n,F,(n,b,k))\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad b=\chi_F(n).
\]
All off-sort relation instances are false.

For a copy \(\mathcal B\cong\mathcal A\) on domain \(\omega\), \(D(\mathcal B)\) denotes the complete atomic diagram of \(\mathcal B\), used as an oracle deciding both atomic and negated atomic facts. The phrase “an individual automorphism \(\rho\) of \(\mathcal B\) is computable relative to \(\mathcal B\)” is used in the standard nonuniform sense: \(\rho\leq_TD(\mathcal B)\), and the Turing index may depend on \(\rho\) and may hardwire finitely many natural numbers from the domain of \(\mathcal B\).

With these conventions, for every copy \(\mathcal B\cong\mathcal A\) and every \(\rho\in\operatorname{Aut}(\mathcal B)\), one has \(\rho\leq_TD(\mathcal B)\). Hence \(\mathcal A\) is computably \(\operatorname{AUT}\)-countable on a cone, with empty/computable cone base.

Also, for every finite tuple \(\bar a\) from \(\mathcal A\), there is an automorphism \(\pi\in\operatorname{Aut}(\mathcal A)\) such that the graph
\[
\{(x,y)\in A^2:\pi(x)=y\}
\]
is not definable over the finite parameter set
\[
\operatorname{rng}(\bar a)\cup\pi[\operatorname{rng}(\bar a)]
\]
by any countable family of existential formulas. The proof even rules out countable families of existential \(L_{\omega_1\omega}\)-formulas with quantifier-free infinitary matrices, so it certainly rules out the narrower ordinary first-order existential interpretation. This is not a relaxation of the original problem; it proves the requested existence theorem, with the additional features of a finite relational language and empty cone base.

## Proof

### 1. Construction and computable presentation

Work first with the abstract disjoint union
\[
A=L\sqcup U\sqcup V,
\]
where
\[
L=\{\ell_n:n\in\omega\},
\qquad
U=[\omega]^{<\omega},
\qquad
V=\{(n,b,k):n,k\in\omega,\ b\in\{0,1\}\}.
\]
For each \(n\), let
\[
P_n=\{(n,b,k):b\in\{0,1\},\ k\in\omega\}.
\]
Thus \(P_n\) is the \(V\)-gadget over the label \(\ell_n\), consisting of two directed \(\omega\)-rays:
\[
R_{n,0}=\{(n,0,k):k\in\omega\},
\qquad
R_{n,1}=\{(n,1,k):k\in\omega\}.
\]

The language has unary predicates \(L,U,V\), binary predicates \(N,I\), and ternary predicates \(C,S,T\). Interpret them as follows.

The unary predicates name the three pieces \(L,U,V\). The relation \(N\) is the successor relation on the label sort:
\[
N(\ell_n,\ell_m)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad m=n+1.
\]
The relation \(C\) records toggling a singleton in a finite set:
\[
C(\ell_n,F,G)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad F,G\in[\omega]^{<\omega}\text{ and }G=F\triangle\{n\}.
\]
The incidence relation \(I\) says which \(V\)-gadget lies over which label:
\[
I(\ell_n,(m,b,k))\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad m=n.
\]
The ray-successor relation \(S\) is:
\[
S(\ell_n,(n,b,k),(n,b,k+1)).
\]
Finally, the side relation \(T\) says that, relative to a finite set \(F\in U\), the selected ray over \(n\) is side \(1\) exactly when \(n\in F\):
\[
T(\ell_n,F,(n,b,k))\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad b=\chi_F(n).
\]
All relation instances outside these intended sorts are declared false.

This structure has a computable presentation on domain \(\omega\). For example, code \(\ell_n\) by a tagged natural number \((0,n)\), code \(F\in[\omega]^{<\omega}\) by \((1,e)\), where \(e=\sum_{i\in F}2^i\), and code \((n,b,k)\in V\) by \((2,n,b,k)\), using any fixed computable pairing/tupling function to identify tagged objects with \(\omega\). In this presentation, the predicates \(L,U,V\) and all relations \(N,I,C,S,T\) are decidable. We identify this computable presentation with \(\mathcal A\).

### 2. The automorphism group

For each finite \(E\subseteq\omega\), define a map \(\alpha_E:A\to A\) by
\[
\alpha_E(\ell_n)=\ell_n,
\]
\[
\alpha_E(F)=F\triangle E\qquad(F\in U),
\]
and
\[
\alpha_E(n,b,k)=(n,b\oplus\chi_E(n),k),
\]
where \(\oplus\) is addition modulo \(2\).

Each \(\alpha_E\) is an automorphism. It preserves the three unary sorts. It fixes every label, so it preserves \(N\) and \(I\). It preserves \(C\), since
\[
G=F\triangle\{n\}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
G\triangle E=(F\triangle E)\triangle\{n\}.
\]
It preserves \(S\), because it only possibly swaps the two rays over a fixed \(n\), without changing the level \(k\). It preserves \(T\), because
\[
\chi_{F\triangle E}(n)=\chi_F(n)\oplus\chi_E(n).
\]

Conversely, let \(\beta\in\operatorname{Aut}(\mathcal A)\). Since \(L\) is named by a unary predicate, \(\beta\) maps \(L\) onto \(L\). The structure \((L,N)\) is a directed \(\omega\)-ray. The element \(\ell_0\) is the unique label with no \(N\)-predecessor, so \(\beta(\ell_0)=\ell_0\). Inductively, if \(\beta(\ell_n)=\ell_n\), then \(\beta(\ell_{n+1})\) must be the unique \(N\)-successor of \(\ell_n\), namely \(\ell_{n+1}\). Hence \(\beta\) fixes every label.

For \(n\in\omega\), let \(\tau_n:U\to U\) be the singleton toggle
\[
\tau_n(F)=F\triangle\{n\}.
\]
The relation \(C\) says exactly that \(C(\ell_n,F,\tau_n(F))\). Since \(\beta\) fixes \(\ell_n\), it commutes with every \(\tau_n\):
\[
\beta(\tau_n(F))=\tau_n(\beta(F)).
\]
Let
\[
E=\beta(\varnothing)\in U.
\]
Because \(U=[\omega]^{<\omega}\), this \(E\) is finite. If \(F=\{n_0,\dots,n_{r-1}\}\), then
\[
F=\tau_{n_0}\cdots\tau_{n_{r-1}}(\varnothing),
\]
so
\[
\beta(F)
=\tau_{n_0}\cdots\tau_{n_{r-1}}(E)
=F\triangle E.
\]
Thus \(\beta\) acts on \(U\) by translation by the finite set \(E\).

Now consider \(V\). Since \(\beta\) fixes labels and preserves \(I\), it maps each gadget
\[
P_n=\{(n,b,k):b\in\{0,1\},\ k\in\omega\}
\]
onto itself. Inside \(P_n\), the relation \(S(\ell_n,\cdot,\cdot)\) is the disjoint union of two directed \(\omega\)-rays. The two roots \((n,0,0)\) and \((n,1,0)\) are exactly the elements of \(P_n\) with no \(S(\ell_n)\)-predecessor. Therefore \(\beta\) either fixes the two roots or swaps them, and uniqueness of successors forces the action to be level-preserving. Hence for each \(n\) there is \(\varepsilon_n\in\{0,1\}\) such that
\[
\beta(n,b,k)=(n,b\oplus\varepsilon_n,k).
\]

Use \(T\) to identify \(\varepsilon_n\). Since
\[
T(\ell_n,\varnothing,(n,0,0))
\]
holds, applying \(\beta\) gives
\[
T(\ell_n,E,(n,\varepsilon_n,0)).
\]
By the definition of \(T\), this is equivalent to
\[
\varepsilon_n=\chi_E(n).
\]
Therefore \(\beta=\alpha_E\).

Thus
\[
\operatorname{Aut}(\mathcal A)=\{\alpha_E:E\in[\omega]^{<\omega}\}.
\]

### 3. Computable \(\operatorname{AUT}\)-countability on a cone

Let \(\mathcal B\cong\mathcal A\) be any copy on domain \(\omega\), and let \(\rho\in\operatorname{Aut}(\mathcal B)\). We use \(D(\mathcal B)\) for the complete atomic diagram of \(\mathcal B\), so an oracle query decides whether any atomic relation holds and, equivalently, whether its negation holds.

The reducibility is nonuniform in \(\rho\): for each particular pair \((\mathcal B,\rho)\), we may choose a Turing index depending on \(\rho\), and the program may hardwire finitely many natural numbers from the domain of \(\mathcal B\).

By transporting the automorphism classification through an isomorphism \(\mathcal A\cong\mathcal B\), the automorphism \(\rho\) has finite support. More explicitly, there are finitely many labels
\[
f_0,\dots,f_{m-1}\in L^{\mathcal B}
\]
such that:

1. \(\rho\) fixes every label in \(L^{\mathcal B}\);
2. on \(U^{\mathcal B}\), \(\rho\) is the product of the \(C\)-toggles at \(f_0,\dots,f_{m-1}\);
3. on \(V^{\mathcal B}\), \(\rho\) swaps the two \(S(f_i,\cdot,\cdot)\)-rays over each \(f_i\), preserving level, and fixes every \(V\)-gadget over a label not among the \(f_i\).

For each supported label \(f_i\), choose and hardwire the two roots
\[
r_i^0,\ r_i^1
\]
of the two \(S(f_i,\cdot,\cdot)\)-rays in its gadget.

We now describe an oracle Turing computation of \(\rho(x)\) from \(D(\mathcal B)\).

Given input \(x\in\omega\), first use \(D(\mathcal B)\) to decide which of \(L(x),U(x),V(x)\) holds.

If \(L(x)\), output \(x\). Every automorphism fixes the label ray pointwise.

If \(U(x)\), set \(y:=x\). For \(i=0,\dots,m-1\), search through \(z=0,1,2,\dots\) until finding the unique \(z\) such that
\[
C(f_i,y,z)
\]
holds in \(\mathcal B\). Replace \(y\) by \(z\), and continue. After all supported labels have been processed, output \(y\). Each search halts because, in every copy of \(\mathcal A\), \(C(f_i,\cdot,\cdot)\) is a total involution on \(U^{\mathcal B}\).

If \(V(x)\), use \(D(\mathcal B)\) to test the finitely many incidence facts
\[
I(f_i,x)\qquad(i<m).
\]
If none holds, then \(x\) lies in a gadget outside the finite support, so output \(x\). This finite negative test is exactly where the incidence relation \(I\) is essential: it lets the machine decide in finite time that a \(V\)-element is outside all supported gadgets.

If \(I(f_i,x)\) holds for some \(i\), then \(x\) lies in the gadget over \(f_i\). Initialize
\[
a:=r_i^0,\qquad b:=r_i^1.
\]
Repeat the following procedure:

- if \(x=a\), output \(b\);
- if \(x=b\), output \(a\);
- otherwise, search for the unique \(a'\) such that \(S(f_i,a,a')\), and the unique \(b'\) such that \(S(f_i,b,b')\);
- replace \(a,b\) by \(a',b'\).

This halts because every element of the gadget over \(f_i\) lies at a finite \(S(f_i)\)-distance from exactly one of the two roots. The output is the element on the opposite ray at the same level, which is precisely \(\rho(x)\).

Thus, for every copy \(\mathcal B\cong\mathcal A\) and every automorphism \(\rho\in\operatorname{Aut}(\mathcal B)\),
\[
\rho\leq_TD(\mathcal B)
\]
in the nonuniform sense. No additional oracle is used, so \(\mathcal A\) is computably \(\operatorname{AUT}\)-countable on a cone with computable cone base \(0\).

### 4. The non-\(\Sigma^{in}_1\)-definability property

Fix a finite tuple \(\bar a\) from \(\mathcal A\). Since \(\bar a\) is finite, only finitely many \(V\)-gadgets \(P_n\) contain entries of \(\bar a\). Choose \(n\in\omega\) such that no entry of \(\bar a\) lies in
\[
P_n=\{(n,b,k):b\in\{0,1\},\ k\in\omega\}.
\]

Let
\[
\pi=\alpha_{\{n\}}.
\]
Thus \(\pi\) fixes every label, sends \(F\in U\) to \(F\triangle\{n\}\), swaps the two rays in \(P_n\), and fixes every \(P_m\) for \(m\neq n\).

Let
\[
Q=\operatorname{rng}(\bar a)\cup\pi[\operatorname{rng}(\bar a)]
\]
be the allowed finite parameter set. Because no entry of \(\bar a\) lies in \(P_n\), and because \(\pi\) sends \(V\)-elements outside \(P_n\) to themselves, the set \(Q\) is disjoint from \(P_n\). The set \(Q\) may contain labels, including possibly \(\ell_n\), and may contain \(U\)-elements whose images reveal \(n\); this is harmless.

Put
\[
r_0=(n,0,0),\qquad r_1=(n,1,0),\qquad s_0=(n,0,1).
\]
Then
\[
\pi(r_0)=r_1,
\]
but
\[
\pi(s_0)=(n,1,1)\neq r_1.
\]

Define a map \(e:A\to A\) by shifting only the side-\(0\) ray of \(P_n\):
\[
e(n,0,k)=(n,0,k+1),
\]
and
\[
e(x)=x
\]
for every other element \(x\in A\).

The map \(e\) is injective and fixes every element of \(Q\), because \(Q\cap P_n=\varnothing\) and \(e\) fixes all labels, all \(U\)-elements, and all \(V\)-elements outside the side-\(0\) ray of \(P_n\).

We claim that \(e\) is a strong self-embedding: for every atomic formula \(\theta\), including equality, and every tuple \(\bar c\),
\[
\mathcal A\models\theta(\bar c)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\mathcal A\models\theta(e\bar c).
\]

Equality is preserved and reflected because \(e\) is injective. The unary predicates \(L,U,V\) are preserved and reflected because \(e\) never changes the sort of an element.

The relation \(N\) is preserved and reflected because \(N\) lives entirely on \(L\), and \(e\) fixes every label.

The relation \(C\) is preserved and reflected because \(C\) lives on \(L\times U\times U\), and \(e\) fixes all elements of \(L\) and \(U\).

The relation \(I\) is preserved and reflected because \(I(\ell_m,x)\) only says that \(x\) belongs to the gadget \(P_m\), and \(e\) never changes the \(V\)-gadget containing an element.

The relation \(T\) is preserved and reflected because \(T(\ell_m,F,(m,b,k))\) depends on \(m\), \(F\), and the side \(b\), but not on the level \(k\); the map \(e\) changes only the level \(k\) on one ray.

It remains to check \(S\). On every ray except \(R_{n,0}\), the map \(e\) is the identity. On \(R_{n,0}\), it sends
\[
(n,0,k)\mapsto(n,0,k+1).
\]
Thus every true \(S\)-edge on \(R_{n,0}\),
\[
(n,0,k)\to(n,0,k+1),
\]
is sent to the true \(S\)-edge
\[
(n,0,k+1)\to(n,0,k+2).
\]
Conversely, if an \(S\)-edge holds between two images on \(R_{n,0}\), its first endpoint cannot be the root \((n,0,0)\), since that root is not in the image of \(e\). Hence the edge is of the form
\[
(n,0,k+1)\to(n,0,k+2),
\]
and comes from the original edge
\[
(n,0,k)\to(n,0,k+1).
\]
So \(S\) is preserved and reflected. Since off-sort relation instances are false and \(e\) preserves and reflects the relevant sorts, all off-sort false instances remain false.

Therefore \(e\) preserves and reflects every atomic formula, including equality. Since \(e\) fixes every parameter in \(Q\), it follows by induction on formulas that every quantifier-free formula over parameters from \(Q\), including formulas with negated atomics and countable Boolean combinations, is preserved by \(e\).

Consequently, every existential formula over \(Q\) true of \((r_0,r_1)\) is true of \((s_0,r_1)\). Indeed, if
\[
\varphi(x,y)\equiv \exists \bar z\,\psi(\bar q,x,y,\bar z)
\]
is existential, where \(\bar q\) is a tuple from \(Q\) and \(\psi\) is quantifier-free, and if
\[
\mathcal A\models\varphi(r_0,r_1),
\]
choose witnesses \(\bar w\) such that
\[
\mathcal A\models\psi(\bar q,r_0,r_1,\bar w).
\]
Applying \(e\), and using \(e(\bar q)=\bar q\), \(e(r_0)=s_0\), and \(e(r_1)=r_1\), gives
\[
\mathcal A\models\psi(\bar q,s_0,r_1,e\bar w).
\]
Hence
\[
\mathcal A\models\varphi(s_0,r_1).
\]

Suppose, toward contradiction, that the graph of \(\pi\) were \(\Sigma^{in}_1\)-definable over \(Q\). Then there would be a countable family of existential formulas
\[
(\varphi_i(x,y))_{i\in\omega}
\]
with parameters from \(Q\) such that, for all \(x,y\in A\),
\[
\pi(x)=y
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\exists i\in\omega\ \mathcal A\models\varphi_i(x,y).
\]
Since \(\pi(r_0)=r_1\), some \(\varphi_i(r_0,r_1)\) holds. By the preservation argument, the same \(\varphi_i(s_0,r_1)\) holds. The alleged definition would therefore imply
\[
\pi(s_0)=r_1.
\]
But actually
\[
\pi(s_0)=(n,1,1)\neq r_1.
\]
This contradiction shows that the graph of \(\pi\) is not \(\Sigma^{in}_1\)-definable over
\[
\operatorname{rng}(\bar a)\cup\pi[\operatorname{rng}(\bar a)].
\]

Since \(\bar a\) was arbitrary, the desired nondefinability property holds for every finite parameter tuple. Combining this with the computable presentation and the cone result proves the theorem.

# Candidate Proof (on the right track, may have gaps or unnecessary jargon)
## Original Problem

We consider infinite countable first-order structures, their automorphism groups and the descriptive aspects of their representations. A representation $\A$ of such a structure is an instance of its isomorphism type in which the universe or domain of the structure is the set of natural numbers $\N$ and the constants, functions and relations of the structure are elements of $\N$, multi-variable functions from $\N$ to $\N$ and multi-place relations on $\N$, respectively.   Similarly, an automorphism of $\A$ or an isomorphism from $\A$ to $\B$ is a bijective function from $\N$ to $\N$ that preserves the evaluation of all constants, functions and relations.  We let $\AUT(\A)$ denote the automorphism group of $\A$.

\begin{definition}\label{1.1}
  \begin{itemize}
  \item     $\A$ is \emph{computably $\AUT$-countable} if every automorphism of $\A$ is computable relative to $\A$.  
  \item  $\A$ is \emph{computably $\AUT$-countable on a cone} if there is a $C\subset\N$ such that whenever $\B$ is isomorphic to $\A$, every automorphism of $\B$ is computable relative to $\B\oplus C$.
  \end{itemize}
\end{definition}


\begin{definition}
  A function $f:\A\to\A$ is \emph{$\Sigma^{in}_1$-definable} in $\A$ relative to parameters
  $\bar{a}=(a_1,\dots,a_n)$ from $\A$ if there is a family of existential formulas $(\phi_i)_{i\in\N}$ such that for all $x$ and $y$ in $\A$, $f(x)=y$ if and only if there is an $i\in\N$ such that $\A\models\phi_i(\bar{a},x,y)$.
\end{definition}

\begin{question}\label{sec:question}\label{1.2}
  Is there a countable computably-represented structure $\A$ such that $\A$ is computably $\AUT$-countable on a cone and such that for any finite  set of parameters $\bar{a}$ in $\A$ there is an automorphism $\pi$ of $\A$ such that $\pi$ is not $\Sigma^{in}_1$-definable in $\A$ relative to the parameters $\bar{a}\cup \pi(\bar{a}),$ where $\pi(\bar{a})$ is the image of $\bar{a}$ under $\pi$.
\end{question}

## Problem Solved

We prove the following fully explicit positive answer.

There exists a first-order structure \(\mathcal A\) with universe \(\omega=\{0,1,2,\dots\}\) in the finite relational language
\[
\tau=\{L,U,V,N,I,C,S,T\},
\]
where \(L,U,V\) are unary relation symbols, \(N,I\) are binary relation symbols, and \(C,S,T\) are ternary relation symbols, such that:

1. **Computable presentation.** Each relation of \(\mathcal A\) is a computable subset of the appropriate finite power of \(\omega\).

2. **Automorphism computability in all copies.** If \(\mathcal B\) is any \(\tau\)-structure with universe \(\omega\) and \(\mathcal B\cong\mathcal A\), and if \(\rho\in\operatorname{Aut}(\mathcal B)\), then \(\rho:\omega\to\omega\) is Turing-computable relative to the complete atomic diagram \(D(\mathcal B)\) of \(\mathcal B\). Here \(D(\mathcal B)\) means an oracle deciding, for every relation symbol \(R\in\tau\) and tuple \(\bar n\in\omega^{\operatorname{arity}(R)}\), whether \(\mathcal B\models R(\bar n)\). The reduction is nonuniform: the Turing-machine index may depend on \(\mathcal B\) and on \(\rho\).

3. **Failure of parameter-restricted \(\Sigma^{in}_1\)-definition.** For every finite tuple \(\bar a\) of elements of \(\mathcal A\), there is an automorphism \(\pi\in\operatorname{Aut}(\mathcal A)\) such that the graph
\[
\operatorname{Graph}(\pi)=\{(x,y)\in A^2:\pi(x)=y\}
\]
is not definable in \(\mathcal A\), using parameters from
\[
\operatorname{rng}(\bar a)\cup \pi[\operatorname{rng}(\bar a)],
\]
by any \(\Sigma^{in}_1\) definition. In this proof, a \(\Sigma^{in}_1\) definition means a countable disjunction of existential \(L_{\omega_1\omega}\)-formulas, each of the form
\[
\exists \bar z\,\theta(\bar p,x,y,\bar z),
\]
where \(\bar z\) is finite, \(\bar p\) is a finite tuple of allowed parameters, and \(\theta\) is quantifier-free, allowing countable conjunctions and disjunctions of atomic and negated atomic formulas.

This is not a relaxation of the original question. It proves the original existence claim, and in fact proves the stronger copy-computability statement with cone base \(C=\emptyset\), in a finite relational language.

## Proof

### 1. The computable structure

Fix a computable pairing function \(\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle:\omega^2\to\omega\) with computable inverse functions.

We code three disjoint sorts inside \(\omega\). For \(l,e,k\in\omega\) and \(b\in\{0,1\}\), define
\[
\ell_l=3l,\qquad u_e=3e+1,\qquad v_{l,b,k}=3\langle l,2k+b\rangle+2.
\]
Thus every natural number is uniquely either some \(\ell_l\), some \(u_e\), or some \(v_{l,b,k}\).

For \(e\in\omega\), let
\[
F_e=\{i\in\omega:\text{the }i\text{th binary digit of }e\text{ is }1\}.
\]
So \(F_e\) is a finite subset of \(\omega\), and every finite subset of \(\omega\) occurs uniquely in this way.

Intuitively:

- \(L=\{\ell_l:l\in\omega\}\) is a rigid label ray.
- \(U=\{u_e:e\in\omega\}\) codes finite subsets of labels.
- \(V=\{v_{l,b,k}:l,k\in\omega,\ b\in\{0,1\}\}\) consists, over each label \(l\), of two directed successor rays:
  \[
  R_{l,0}=\{v_{l,0,k}:k\in\omega\},\qquad
  R_{l,1}=\{v_{l,1,k}:k\in\omega\}.
  \]

Let
\[
P_l=R_{l,0}\cup R_{l,1}.
\]

Define the relations of \(\mathcal A\) as follows.

- \(L(x)\) iff \(x=\ell_l\) for some \(l\).
- \(U(x)\) iff \(x=u_e\) for some \(e\).
- \(V(x)\) iff \(x=v_{l,b,k}\) for some \(l,b,k\).
- \(N(\ell_l,\ell_m)\) iff \(m=l+1\).
- \(C(\ell_l,u_e,u_{e'})\) iff \(F_{e'}=F_e\triangle\{l\}\), where \(\triangle\) denotes symmetric difference.
- \(I(\ell_l,v_{m,b,k})\) iff \(l=m\).
- \(S(\ell_l,v_{l,b,k},v_{l,b,k+1})\) holds for all \(l,b,k\).
- \(T(\ell_l,u_e,v_{m,b,k})\) iff \(m=l\) and \(b=1\) exactly when \(l\in F_e\).

All relations are false outside these intended sorts.

Equivalently, \(C\) toggles the \(l\)th bit of a finite subset; \(I\) says that a \(V\)-element belongs to the gadget over label \(l\); \(S\) is the directed successor relation on each ray; and \(T\) says that the side \(b\in\{0,1\}\) of a point in \(P_l\) agrees with whether \(l\) belongs to the finite set coded by \(u_e\).

Because congruence modulo \(3\), the pairing function, its inverse functions, and binary-bit operations are computable, each relation above is decidable. Thus \(\mathcal A\) is a computable presentation with domain \(\omega\) in a finite relational language.

### 2. Classification of automorphisms of \(\mathcal A\)

For each finite set \(F\subseteq\omega\), define a map \(\alpha_F:A\to A\) by
\[
\alpha_F(\ell_l)=\ell_l,
\]
\[
\alpha_F(u_e)=u_{e'},
\quad\text{where }F_{e'}=F_e\triangle F,
\]
and
\[
\alpha_F(v_{l,b,k})=v_{l,b\oplus \chi_F(l),k},
\]
where \(\oplus\) denotes addition modulo \(2\), and \(\chi_F(l)=1\) iff \(l\in F\).

We first check that every \(\alpha_F\) is an automorphism.

The sort predicates \(L,U,V\) are preserved. The relation \(N\) is preserved because all labels are fixed. The incidence relation \(I\) is preserved because \(\alpha_F\) does not change the label coordinate \(l\) of \(v_{l,b,k}\).

For \(C\), if
\[
C(\ell_l,u_e,u_{e'})
\]
holds, then \(F_{e'}=F_e\triangle\{l\}\). Applying \(\alpha_F\) sends \(u_e\) to the code for \(F_e\triangle F\), and \(u_{e'}\) to the code for
\[
F_{e'}\triangle F=(F_e\triangle\{l\})\triangle F=(F_e\triangle F)\triangle\{l\}.
\]
So \(C\) is preserved.

For \(S\), \(\alpha_F\) either fixes both rays over \(l\) or swaps them, but it always preserves the level \(k\). Hence
\[
S(\ell_l,v_{l,b,k},v_{l,b,k+1})
\]
is carried to
\[
S(\ell_l,v_{l,b\oplus\chi_F(l),k},v_{l,b\oplus\chi_F(l),k+1}).
\]

For \(T\), if \(T(\ell_l,u_e,v_{l,b,k})\) holds, then \(b=\chi_{F_e}(l)\). After applying \(\alpha_F\), the \(U\)-element codes \(F_e\triangle F\), and the side becomes
\[
b\oplus\chi_F(l)=\chi_{F_e}(l)\oplus\chi_F(l)=\chi_{F_e\triangle F}(l).
\]
Thus \(T\) is preserved. Therefore each \(\alpha_F\) is an automorphism.

Now let \(\beta\in\operatorname{Aut}(\mathcal A)\). We show that \(\beta=\alpha_F\) for a unique finite \(F\subseteq\omega\).

#### Labels are fixed pointwise

Since \(L\) is a unary predicate, \(\beta\) maps \(L\) onto \(L\). Inside \(L\), the element \(\ell_0\) is the unique element with no \(N\)-predecessor. Hence \(\beta(\ell_0)=\ell_0\). If \(\beta(\ell_l)=\ell_l\), then \(\ell_{l+1}\) is the unique \(N\)-successor of \(\ell_l\), so \(\beta(\ell_{l+1})=\ell_{l+1}\). By induction, \(\beta\) fixes every \(\ell_l\).

#### The action on \(U\) is finite symmetric-difference translation

For each \(l\), let \(\tau_l\) be the involution on \(U\) given by
\[
\tau_l(u_e)=u_{e'},
\quad F_{e'}=F_e\triangle\{l\}.
\]
The relation \(C\) says exactly that \(C(\ell_l,u,\tau_l(u))\). Since \(\beta\) fixes \(\ell_l\), preserves \(C\), and \(C(\ell_l,u,\cdot)\) has a unique value, we have
\[
\beta(\tau_l(u))=\tau_l(\beta(u))
\]
for every \(u\in U\).

Let \(\beta(u_0)=u_f\), where \(F_f\) is finite. Put \(F=F_f\). Every \(u_e\in U\) is obtained from \(u_0\) by finitely many singleton toggles. Therefore
\[
\beta(u_e)=u_{e'},
\quad F_{e'}=F_e\triangle F.
\]

#### The action on \(V\) is forced by \(T\)

Since \(I\) is preserved and labels are fixed, \(\beta\) maps each \(P_l\) onto itself. The \(S(\ell_l,\cdot,\cdot)\)-structure on \(P_l\) is exactly two directed \(\omega\)-rays:
\[
R_{l,0},\qquad R_{l,1}.
\]
The two roots \(v_{l,0,0}\) and \(v_{l,1,0}\) are the only elements of \(P_l\) with no \(S(\ell_l)\)-predecessor. Thus \(\beta\) either fixes the two rays or swaps them. Since each directed \(\omega\)-ray is rigid, there is some \(\epsilon_l\in\{0,1\}\) such that
\[
\beta(v_{l,b,k})=v_{l,b\oplus\epsilon_l,k}
\]
for all \(b,k\).

Now use \(T\) with \(u_0\). We have
\[
T(\ell_l,u_0,v_{l,0,0}).
\]
Applying \(\beta\), and using \(\beta(u_0)=u_f\), gives
\[
T(\ell_l,u_f,v_{l,\epsilon_l,0}).
\]
By the definition of \(T\), this means
\[
\epsilon_l=\chi_F(l).
\]
Thus
\[
\beta(v_{l,b,k})=v_{l,b\oplus\chi_F(l),k}.
\]

Combining the three parts, \(\beta=\alpha_F\). Therefore
\[
\operatorname{Aut}(\mathcal A)=\{\alpha_F:F\subseteq\omega\text{ finite}\}.
\]

### 3. Every automorphism of every copy is computable from that copy

Let \(\mathcal B\cong\mathcal A\) be any copy with universe \(\omega\), and let \(\rho\in\operatorname{Aut}(\mathcal B)\).

Choose an isomorphism \(h:\mathcal A\to\mathcal B\). Then
\[
h^{-1}\rho h\in\operatorname{Aut}(\mathcal A),
\]
so by the classification above,
\[
h^{-1}\rho h=\alpha_F
\]
for some finite \(F\subseteq\omega\).

Let
\[
F_B=\{h(\ell_l):l\in F\}.
\]
This is the finite set of support labels in \(\mathcal B\). For each \(f=h(\ell_l)\in F_B\), hardwire into the Turing program the two roots
\[
r_f^0=h(v_{l,0,0}),\qquad r_f^1=h(v_{l,1,0})
\]
of the two \(S(f)\)-rays in the gadget over \(f\).

This finite hardwiring is legitimate for ordinary nonuniform Turing reducibility: for each fixed \(\mathcal B\) and each fixed \(\rho\), the program computing \(\rho\) may have finitely many natural numbers built into its index.

We now describe an oracle computation of \(\rho(x)\) using \(D(\mathcal B)\).

#### Case 1: \(x\in L^\mathcal B\)

If \(D(\mathcal B)\) says \(L(x)\), output \(x\). Every automorphism fixes the label ray pointwise.

#### Case 2: \(x\in U^\mathcal B\)

If \(D(\mathcal B)\) says \(U(x)\), set \(y=x\). For each hardwired support label \(f\in F_B\), search through \(z=0,1,2,\dots\) until finding the unique \(z\) such that
\[
\mathcal B\models C(f,y,z).
\]
Then replace \(y\) by this \(z\). After doing this for all \(f\in F_B\), output \(y\).

This halts because in every copy, for each label \(f\) and each \(U\)-element \(y\), there is a unique \(C(f,y,\cdot)\)-successor.

This computes the \(U\)-part of \(\rho\), because \(\rho\) acts on \(U^\mathcal B\) by toggling exactly the finitely many labels in \(F_B\).

#### Case 3: \(x\in V^\mathcal B\)

If \(D(\mathcal B)\) says \(V(x)\), first test the finitely many support labels \(f\in F_B\). For each such \(f\), query whether
\[
\mathcal B\models I(f,x).
\]

If no support label \(f\in F_B\) satisfies \(I(f,x)\), output \(x\). This is correct because \(\rho\) fixes every gadget not in its finite support.

If \(I(f,x)\) holds for some support label \(f\), then \(x\) lies in the gadget over \(f\), whose two roots \(r_f^0,r_f^1\) have been hardwired. Starting with
\[
y_0=r_f^0,\qquad y_1=r_f^1,
\]
iterate along the two \(S(f)\)-rays in parallel:

- At each stage, if \(x=y_0\), output \(y_1\).
- If \(x=y_1\), output \(y_0\).
- Otherwise, search for the unique \(S(f)\)-successor \(y_0'\) of \(y_0\), and the unique \(S(f)\)-successor \(y_1'\) of \(y_1\), then replace \(y_0,y_1\) by \(y_0',y_1'\).

This halts because every element of the gadget over \(f\) lies at a finite distance from exactly one of the two roots. It outputs the point at the same level on the other ray, which is exactly how \(\rho\) acts on a supported gadget.

Thus for every copy \(\mathcal B\cong\mathcal A\) and every \(\rho\in\operatorname{Aut}(\mathcal B)\),
\[
\rho\leq_T D(\mathcal B).
\]
So the cone base may be taken to be empty.

### 4. The nondefinability argument

Let \(\bar a\) be any finite tuple from \(\mathcal A\). Since only finitely many \(V\)-elements occur in \(\bar a\), choose \(l\in\omega\) such that no entry of \(\bar a\) lies in \(P_l\).

Let
\[
\pi=\alpha_{\{l\}}.
\]
Then \(\pi\) swaps the two rays over \(l\), toggles the \(l\)th bit on \(U\), fixes all labels, and fixes every gadget \(P_m\) for \(m\neq l\). Since \(\bar a\) contains no element of \(P_l\), the finite parameter set
\[
p=\operatorname{rng}(\bar a)\cup \pi[\operatorname{rng}(\bar a)]
\]
also contains no element of \(P_l\).

Let
\[
r_0=v_{l,0,0},\qquad r_1=v_{l,1,0},\qquad s_0=v_{l,0,1}.
\]
Then
\[
\pi(r_0)=r_1,
\]
but
\[
\pi(s_0)=v_{l,1,1}\neq r_1.
\]

We will show that no \(\Sigma^{in}_1\) definition over parameters from \(p\) can distinguish the true graph pair \((r_0,r_1)\) from the false pair \((s_0,r_1)\).

Define a map \(e:A\to A\) by shifting the \(0\)-side of the \(l\)-gadget forward by one step and fixing everything else:
\[
e(v_{l,0,k})=v_{l,0,k+1},
\]
and
\[
e(x)=x
\]
for every other element \(x\).

Then \(e\) is injective, fixes every element of \(p\), sends \(r_0\) to \(s_0\), and fixes \(r_1\).

#### The map \(e\) is a strong self-embedding

We claim that for every atomic formula \(\theta(\bar x)\), including equality,
\[
\mathcal A\models \theta(\bar c)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\mathcal A\models \theta(e(\bar c)).
\]

Equality is preserved and reflected because \(e\) is injective.

The unary predicates \(L,U,V\) are preserved and reflected because \(e\) preserves the three sorts. The relations \(N\) and \(C\) are preserved and reflected because \(e\) fixes all \(L\)- and \(U\)-elements.

The relation \(I\) is preserved and reflected because \(e\) never changes the label coordinate of a \(V\)-element.

The relation \(T\) is preserved and reflected because \(T(\ell_m,u,v_{m,b,k})\) depends only on the label \(m\), the \(U\)-element \(u\), and the side \(b\), and \(e\) changes none of these.

Finally, consider \(S\). The only part of the structure moved by \(e\) is the ray
\[
v_{l,0,0}\to v_{l,0,1}\to v_{l,0,2}\to\cdots.
\]
On this ray, \(e\) is the successor map \(k\mapsto k+1\), which preserves and reflects adjacency:
\[
S(\ell_l,v_{l,0,k},v_{l,0,k+1})
\]
holds iff
\[
S(\ell_l,v_{l,0,k+1},v_{l,0,k+2})
\]
holds. Outside this ray, \(e\) is the identity, and there are no \(S\)-edges between different rays. Hence \(S\) is preserved and reflected.

Thus \(e\) is a strong self-embedding of \(\mathcal A\).

Because \(e\) preserves and reflects all atomic and negated atomic formulas, and fixes every parameter in \(p\), a straightforward induction on quantifier-free \(L_{\omega_1\omega}\)-formulas gives:

For every quantifier-free infinitary formula \(\theta\) with parameters from \(p\), and every finite tuple \(\bar c\),
\[
\mathcal A\models \theta(\bar c)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\mathcal A\models \theta(e(\bar c)).
\]

#### Existential formulas transfer from the true pair to the false pair

Let
\[
\varphi(x,y)
\]
be any existential \(L_{\omega_1\omega}\)-formula with parameters from \(p\). Thus
\[
\varphi(x,y)\equiv \exists \bar z\,\theta(x,y,\bar z,\bar p),
\]
where \(\bar z\) is finite, \(\bar p\) is a tuple from \(p\), and \(\theta\) is quantifier-free infinitary.

Suppose
\[
\mathcal A\models \varphi(r_0,r_1).
\]
Choose witnesses \(\bar c\) such that
\[
\mathcal A\models \theta(r_0,r_1,\bar c,\bar p).
\]
Applying the quantifier-free preservation result for \(e\), and using that \(e(r_0)=s_0\), \(e(r_1)=r_1\), and \(e\) fixes \(\bar p\), we obtain
\[
\mathcal A\models \theta(s_0,r_1,e(\bar c),\bar p).
\]
Therefore
\[
\mathcal A\models \varphi(s_0,r_1).
\]

So every existential formula over \(p\) true of the genuine graph pair \((r_0,r_1)\) is also true of the false pair \((s_0,r_1)\).

#### No countable disjunction of existential formulas defines the graph

Assume, toward contradiction, that \(\operatorname{Graph}(\pi)\) is \(\Sigma^{in}_1\)-definable over parameters from \(p\). Then there is a countable family of existential formulas
\[
(\varphi_i(x,y))_{i\in\omega}
\]
with parameters from \(p\) such that for all \(x,y\in A\),
\[
\pi(x)=y
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\mathcal A\models \bigvee_{i\in\omega}\varphi_i(x,y).
\]

Since \(\pi(r_0)=r_1\), there is some \(i\) such that
\[
\mathcal A\models \varphi_i(r_0,r_1).
\]
By the transfer argument above,
\[
\mathcal A\models \varphi_i(s_0,r_1).
\]
Hence
\[
\mathcal A\models \bigvee_{i\in\omega}\varphi_i(s_0,r_1),
\]
so the alleged definition says \(\pi(s_0)=r_1\). But
\[
\pi(s_0)=v_{l,1,1}\neq r_1.
\]
This contradiction shows that \(\operatorname{Graph}(\pi)\) is not \(\Sigma^{in}_1\)-definable over parameters from
\[
\operatorname{rng}(\bar a)\cup \pi[\operatorname{rng}(\bar a)].
\]

Since \(\bar a\) was arbitrary, the required nondefinability property holds for every finite tuple of parameters. This completes the proof.

# Instructions

This approach seems to be quite promising. Can you make this approach work to solve the original problem (even if the quantitative bounds are slightly worse)? I want you to be able to get an end-to-end solution without using unnecessary jargon and/or any gaps. No need to optimize constants. The proof should be correct.

Output the complete polished proof, including proper citations, in markdown with inline LaTeX math, ready to be turned into a self-contained LaTeX document.
response:
## A self-contained positive solution

We prove a slightly stronger statement than the one asked for: the cone base can be taken to be the empty set, and the language can be finite and relational. The proof is self-contained; all facts used below are proved in the text.

### Theorem

There is a computable structure \(\mathcal A\) with domain \(\omega\) in a finite relational language such that:

1. For every copy \(\mathcal B\cong\mathcal A\) with domain \(\omega\), every automorphism of \(\mathcal B\) is computable relative to the atomic diagram of \(\mathcal B\). Thus \(\mathcal A\) is computably \(\operatorname{AUT}\)-countable on a cone, with cone base \(\emptyset\).

2. For every finite tuple \(\bar a\) from \(\mathcal A\), there is an automorphism \(\pi\in\operatorname{Aut}(\mathcal A)\) such that \(\pi\) is not \(\Sigma^{in}_1\)-definable in \(\mathcal A\) using parameters from
   \[
   \operatorname{rng}(\bar a)\cup \pi[\operatorname{rng}(\bar a)].
   \]

We use the following convention about formulas. An existential formula means a formula of the form \(\exists \bar z\,\theta\), where \(\bar z\) is finite and \(\theta\) is quantifier-free. The nondefinability argument below works even if \(\theta\) is allowed to be an infinitary quantifier-free \(L_{\omega_1\omega}\)-formula, built from atomic formulas by negation and countable conjunctions and disjunctions. Therefore it applies in particular to the narrower first-order reading of the definition in the problem.

---

## 1. Construction of the computable structure

Fix a computable pairing function \(\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle:\omega^2\to\omega\) with computable projections.

For each \(e\in\omega\), let
\[
F_e=\{i\in\omega:\text{the }i\text{th binary digit of }e\text{ is }1\}.
\]
Thus \(F_e\) is a finite subset of \(\omega\), and every finite subset of \(\omega\) is equal to \(F_e\) for a unique \(e\).

We divide \(\omega\) into three computable sorts. For \(l,e,k\in\omega\) and \(b\in\{0,1\}\), define
\[
\lambda_l=3l,\qquad u_e=3e+1,\qquad v_{l,b,k}=3\langle l,2k+b\rangle+2.
\]
Every natural number is uniquely of one of these three forms.

The language is the finite relational language
\[
\tau=\{L,U,V,N,I,Q,S,T\},
\]
where \(L,U,V\) are unary, \(N,I\) are binary, and \(Q,S,T\) are ternary.

Intuitively:

- \(L=\{\lambda_l:l\in\omega\}\) is a rigid label ray.
- \(U=\{u_e:e\in\omega\}\) codes finite subsets of the label set.
- Over each label \(l\), the \(V\)-sort contains two successor rays
  \[
  R_{l,0}=\{v_{l,0,k}:k\in\omega\},\qquad R_{l,1}=\{v_{l,1,k}:k\in\omega\}.
  \]
  Put
  \[
  P_l=R_{l,0}\cup R_{l,1}.
  \]

Define the relations of \(\mathcal A\) as follows.

1. Sort predicates:
   \[
   L(\lambda_l),\qquad U(u_e),\qquad V(v_{l,b,k})
   \]
   hold for all relevant indices, and the sort predicates are false elsewhere.

2. The label successor relation:
   \[
   N(\lambda_l,\lambda_m)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad m=l+1.
   \]

3. The incidence relation between labels and \(V\)-gadgets:
   \[
   I(\lambda_l,v_{m,b,k})\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad l=m.
   \]

4. The finite-set toggle relation:
   \[
   Q(\lambda_l,u_e,u_{e'})\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad F_{e'}=F_e\triangle\{l\},
   \]
   where \(\triangle\) denotes symmetric difference.

5. The successor relation on the two rays over each label:
   \[
   S(\lambda_l,v_{l,b,k},v_{l,b,k+1})
   \]
   holds for all \(l,b,k\), and \(S\) is false on all other triples.

6. The side-selection relation:
   \[
   T(\lambda_l,u_e,v_{m,b,k})
   \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
   m=l\text{ and }b=\chi_{F_e}(l),
   \]
   where \(\chi_{F_e}(l)=1\) if \(l\in F_e\), and \(\chi_{F_e}(l)=0\) otherwise.

All relations are computable. Indeed, the three sorts are computable from congruence modulo \(3\), the coordinates of \(v_{l,b,k}\) are computably recoverable using the pairing projections and parity, and the finite-set operations \(F_e\mapsto F_e\triangle\{l\}\) and \(l\in F_e\) are computable from binary notation. Therefore \(\mathcal A\) is a computable structure with domain \(\omega\).

---

## 2. Classification of the automorphisms of \(\mathcal A\)

For every finite set \(F\subseteq\omega\), define a map \(\alpha_F:A\to A\) by
\[
\alpha_F(\lambda_l)=\lambda_l,
\]
\[
\alpha_F(u_e)=u_{e'},
\quad\text{where }F_{e'}=F_e\triangle F,
\]
and
\[
\alpha_F(v_{l,b,k})=v_{l,b\oplus\chi_F(l),k},
\]
where \(\oplus\) is addition modulo \(2\).

Thus \(\alpha_F\) fixes all labels, toggles the coded finite set on \(U\) by symmetric difference with \(F\), and swaps the two rays over exactly those labels \(l\in F\).

### Lemma 2.1

For every finite \(F\subseteq\omega\), \(\alpha_F\in\operatorname{Aut}(\mathcal A)\).

#### Proof

The map \(\alpha_F\) is a bijection, and in fact an involution. It clearly preserves the three sorts \(L,U,V\). Since all labels are fixed, \(N\) is preserved.

The relation \(I\) is preserved because \(\alpha_F\) does not change the label coordinate of a \(V\)-element.

The relation \(S\) is preserved because, over a fixed label \(l\), \(\alpha_F\) either fixes both rays pointwise level-by-level or swaps them level-by-level. In either case,
\[
S(\lambda_l,v_{l,b,k},v_{l,b,k+1})
\]
holds if and only if
\[
S(\lambda_l,v_{l,b\oplus\chi_F(l),k},v_{l,b\oplus\chi_F(l),k+1})
\]
holds.

For \(Q\), suppose
\[
Q(\lambda_l,u_e,u_{e'})
\]
holds. Then
\[
F_{e'}=F_e\triangle\{l\}.
\]
After applying \(\alpha_F\), the two \(U\)-elements code
\[
F_e\triangle F
\quad\text{and}\quad
F_{e'}\triangle F.
\]
But
\[
F_{e'}\triangle F
=
(F_e\triangle\{l\})\triangle F
=
(F_e\triangle F)\triangle\{l\}.
\]
Hence \(Q\) is preserved.

For \(T\), suppose
\[
T(\lambda_l,u_e,v_{m,b,k})
\]
holds. Then \(m=l\) and \(b=\chi_{F_e}(l)\). After applying \(\alpha_F\), the \(U\)-element codes \(F_e\triangle F\), and the side of the \(V\)-element becomes
\[
b\oplus\chi_F(l)
=
\chi_{F_e}(l)\oplus\chi_F(l)
=
\chi_{F_e\triangle F}(l).
\]
Thus \(T\) is preserved. Since \(\alpha_F\) is a bijection and an involution, preservation is equivalent to preservation and reflection. Therefore \(\alpha_F\) is an automorphism. \(\square\)

### Lemma 2.2

Every automorphism of \(\mathcal A\) is equal to \(\alpha_F\) for a unique finite set \(F\subseteq\omega\).

#### Proof

Let \(\beta\in\operatorname{Aut}(\mathcal A)\).

First, \(\beta\) fixes every label. The element \(\lambda_0\) is the unique \(L\)-element with no \(N\)-predecessor. Hence \(\beta(\lambda_0)=\lambda_0\). If \(\beta(\lambda_l)=\lambda_l\), then \(\lambda_{l+1}\) is the unique \(N\)-successor of \(\lambda_l\), so \(\beta(\lambda_{l+1})=\lambda_{l+1}\). By induction,
\[
\beta(\lambda_l)=\lambda_l
\]
for every \(l\).

Next consider the action on \(U\). For each \(l\), let \(\tau_l:U\to U\) be the toggle map
\[
\tau_l(u_e)=u_{e'},
\quad\text{where }F_{e'}=F_e\triangle\{l\}.
\]
The relation \(Q\) says exactly that
\[
Q(\lambda_l,u,\tau_l(u))
\]
holds. Since \(\beta\) fixes \(\lambda_l\) and preserves \(Q\), we have
\[
\beta(\tau_l(u))=\tau_l(\beta(u))
\]
for every \(u\in U\).

Let
\[
\beta(u_0)=u_d,
\]
and put
\[
F=F_d.
\]
If \(u_e\in U\), then \(F_e\) is finite, say
\[
F_e=\{l_0,\dots,l_{n-1}\}.
\]
Then
\[
u_e=\tau_{l_0}\cdots\tau_{l_{n-1}}(u_0).
\]
Using the commutation relation above,
\[
\beta(u_e)
=
\tau_{l_0}\cdots\tau_{l_{n-1}}(\beta(u_0))
=
\tau_{l_0}\cdots\tau_{l_{n-1}}(u_d).
\]
Therefore \(\beta(u_e)=u_{e'}\), where
\[
F_{e'}=F_e\triangle F.
\]

It remains to analyze \(V\). Since \(\beta\) fixes every \(\lambda_l\) and preserves \(I\), it maps each gadget
\[
P_l=R_{l,0}\cup R_{l,1}
\]
onto itself. On \(P_l\), the relation \(S(\lambda_l,\cdot,\cdot)\) is exactly two disjoint directed \(\omega\)-rays:
\[
R_{l,0}:v_{l,0,0}\to v_{l,0,1}\to v_{l,0,2}\to\cdots,
\]
\[
R_{l,1}:v_{l,1,0}\to v_{l,1,1}\to v_{l,1,2}\to\cdots.
\]
The roots \(v_{l,0,0}\) and \(v_{l,1,0}\) are precisely the two elements of \(P_l\) with no \(S(\lambda_l)\)-predecessor. Hence \(\beta\) either fixes the two rays or swaps them. Once the image of a root is chosen, the rest of the ray is forced by preservation of the unique successor relation. Thus for each \(l\), there is some \(\epsilon_l\in\{0,1\}\) such that
\[
\beta(v_{l,b,k})=v_{l,b\oplus\epsilon_l,k}
\]
for all \(b,k\).

We now determine \(\epsilon_l\). Since \(u_0\) codes the empty set, we have
\[
T(\lambda_l,u_0,v_{l,0,0})
\]
for every \(l\). Applying \(\beta\), using \(\beta(\lambda_l)=\lambda_l\) and \(\beta(u_0)=u_d\), gives
\[
T(\lambda_l,u_d,v_{l,\epsilon_l,0}).
\]
By the definition of \(T\), this means
\[
\epsilon_l=\chi_{F_d}(l)=\chi_F(l).
\]
Therefore
\[
\beta(v_{l,b,k})=v_{l,b\oplus\chi_F(l),k}.
\]

Combining the descriptions on \(L,U,V\), we get
\[
\beta=\alpha_F.
\]
The set \(F\) is unique because \(F=F_d\), where \(\beta(u_0)=u_d\). \(\square\)

Thus
\[
\operatorname{Aut}(\mathcal A)=\{\alpha_F:F\subseteq\omega\text{ finite}\}.
\]
In particular, \(\operatorname{Aut}(\mathcal A)\) is countable, and in the standard computable presentation every automorphism is computable.

---

## 3. Automorphisms of arbitrary copies are computable from the copy

We now prove the stronger “on a cone” statement with cone base \(\emptyset\).

Let \(\mathcal B\cong\mathcal A\) be any copy with domain \(\omega\), and let
\[
\rho\in\operatorname{Aut}(\mathcal B).
\]
Write \(D(\mathcal B)\) for the complete atomic diagram of \(\mathcal B\), equivalently, in this finite relational language, the join of the characteristic functions of the finitely many relations of \(\mathcal B\).

Choose some isomorphism
\[
h:\mathcal A\to\mathcal B.
\]
Then
\[
h^{-1}\rho h\in\operatorname{Aut}(\mathcal A),
\]
so by Lemma 2.2, there is a finite set \(F\subseteq\omega\) such that
\[
h^{-1}\rho h=\alpha_F.
\]

Let
\[
M=\{h(\lambda_l):l\in F\}.
\]
This is the finite set of labels in \(\mathcal B\) whose two \(S\)-rays are swapped by \(\rho\).

For each \(f=h(\lambda_l)\in M\), fix and hardwire into the program the two roots
\[
r_f^0=h(v_{l,0,0}),\qquad r_f^1=h(v_{l,1,0})
\]
of the two rays over \(f\).

This finite hardwiring is legitimate: to say \(\rho\leq_T D(\mathcal B)\) means that there exists an oracle program computing \(\rho\) from \(D(\mathcal B)\), and the program index may depend on the particular pair \((\mathcal B,\rho)\). Uniformity in \(\rho\) is not part of the definition.

We now describe the oracle computation of \(\rho(x)\) from \(D(\mathcal B)\).

### Case 1: \(x\in L^\mathcal B\)

Query \(L^\mathcal B(x)\). If true, output
\[
x.
\]
This is correct because every automorphism fixes the label ray pointwise.

### Case 2: \(x\in U^\mathcal B\)

If \(U^\mathcal B(x)\), set \(y=x\). For each hardwired support label \(f\in M\), search through \(z=0,1,2,\dots\) until finding the unique \(z\) such that
\[
\mathcal B\models Q(f,y,z).
\]
Then replace \(y\) by \(z\), and continue to the next \(f\in M\). After all support labels have been used, output the resulting \(y\).

This computation halts because, in every copy of \(\mathcal A\), for each label \(f\) and each \(U\)-element \(y\), there is a unique \(z\) satisfying \(Q(f,y,z)\). The effect is to toggle precisely the finitely many labels in \(M\), which is exactly the action of \(\rho\) on \(U^\mathcal B\).

### Case 3: \(x\in V^\mathcal B\)

If \(V^\mathcal B(x)\), first check the finitely many labels \(f\in M\).

If no \(f\in M\) satisfies
\[
\mathcal B\models I(f,x),
\]
then output
\[
x.
\]
This is correct because \(\rho\) fixes every \(V\)-gadget whose label is not in the finite support set \(M\).

Otherwise, there is a unique \(f\in M\) such that
\[
\mathcal B\models I(f,x).
\]
The element \(x\) lies on one of the two rays over \(f\), and \(\rho\) swaps those two rays level-by-level.

Start with the hardwired roots
\[
y_0=r_f^0,\qquad y_1=r_f^1.
\]
Proceed in stages:

- If \(x=y_0\), output \(y_1\).
- If \(x=y_1\), output \(y_0\).
- Otherwise, search for the unique successors \(y_0'\) and \(y_1'\) satisfying
  \[
  \mathcal B\models S(f,y_0,y_0')
  \]
  and
  \[
  \mathcal B\models S(f,y_1,y_1').
  \]
  Replace \(y_0,y_1\) by \(y_0',y_1'\), and repeat.

This halts because every element of the gadget over \(f\) is at some finite level on exactly one of the two rays. The output is the element at the same level on the other ray, which is exactly \(\rho(x)\).

Thus for every copy \(\mathcal B\cong\mathcal A\) and every automorphism \(\rho\in\operatorname{Aut}(\mathcal B)\),
\[
\rho\leq_T D(\mathcal B).
\]
Therefore \(\mathcal A\) is computably \(\operatorname{AUT}\)-countable on a cone with cone base \(\emptyset\).

---

## 4. The nondefinability argument

Let \(\bar a\) be any finite tuple from \(\mathcal A\). We will find an automorphism \(\pi\) such that \(\pi\) is not \(\Sigma^{in}_1\)-definable using parameters from
\[
\operatorname{rng}(\bar a)\cup \pi[\operatorname{rng}(\bar a)].
\]

Only finitely many entries of \(\bar a\) can lie in the \(V\)-sort, and each \(V\)-element lies in exactly one gadget \(P_l\). Choose \(l\in\omega\) such that no entry of \(\bar a\) lies in \(P_l\).

Let
\[
\pi=\alpha_{\{l\}}.
\]
Thus \(\pi\) swaps the two rays over label \(l\), toggles the \(l\)th bit on \(U\), and fixes every \(P_m\) with \(m\neq l\).

Because no entry of \(\bar a\) lies in \(P_l\), and because \(\pi\) sends elements of \(P_m\) to \(P_m\), the finite parameter set
\[
p=\operatorname{rng}(\bar a)\cup \pi[\operatorname{rng}(\bar a)]
\]
contains no element of \(P_l\). It may contain labels, including possibly \(\lambda_l\), and it may contain \(U\)-elements. That will not matter.

Now set
\[
r_0=v_{l,0,0},\qquad r_1=v_{l,1,0},\qquad s_0=v_{l,0,1}.
\]
Then
\[
\pi(r_0)=r_1,
\]
but
\[
\pi(s_0)=v_{l,1,1}\neq r_1.
\]

We will show that every existential formula over parameters from \(p\) that is true of the genuine graph pair \((r_0,r_1)\) is also true of the false pair \((s_0,r_1)\). This will rule out every countable disjunction of existential formulas as a definition of the graph of \(\pi\).

### 4.1. The shift map

Define a map \(\eta:A\to A\) by shifting the \(0\)-side ray over \(l\) forward one step and fixing everything else:
\[
\eta(v_{l,0,k})=v_{l,0,k+1}
\]
for all \(k\), and
\[
\eta(x)=x
\]
for all other \(x\in A\).

Then \(\eta\) is injective, fixes every element of \(p\), sends
\[
\eta(r_0)=s_0,
\]
and fixes
\[
\eta(r_1)=r_1.
\]

The key point is that \(\eta\) preserves and reflects all atomic facts.

### Lemma 4.1

For every atomic formula \(\theta(\bar x)\), allowing equality, and every tuple \(\bar c\) from \(A\),
\[
\mathcal A\models \theta(\bar c)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\mathcal A\models \theta(\eta(\bar c)).
\]

#### Proof

Equality is preserved and reflected because \(\eta\) is injective.

The unary predicates \(L,U,V\) are preserved and reflected because \(\eta\) preserves the three sorts.

The relation \(N\) is preserved and reflected because \(\eta\) fixes all label elements.

The relation \(Q\) is preserved and reflected because \(\eta\) fixes all label elements and all \(U\)-elements.

The relation \(I\) is preserved and reflected because \(\eta\) never changes the label coordinate of a \(V\)-element.

The relation \(T\) is preserved and reflected because \(T(\lambda_m,u_e,v_{m,b,k})\) depends only on the label \(m\), the \(U\)-element \(u_e\), and the side \(b\), not on the level \(k\). The map \(\eta\) fixes labels and \(U\)-elements and, on \(V\), does not change the label coordinate or the side.

Finally consider \(S\). On every \(S\)-ray except \(R_{l,0}\), the map \(\eta\) is the identity. On \(R_{l,0}\), it is the successor map
\[
v_{l,0,k}\mapsto v_{l,0,k+1}.
\]
For \(k,j\in\omega\),
\[
S(\lambda_l,v_{l,0,k},v_{l,0,j})
\]
holds if and only if \(j=k+1\), and this is equivalent to
\[
S(\lambda_l,v_{l,0,k+1},v_{l,0,j+1}).
\]
There are no \(S\)-edges between distinct rays. Hence \(S\) is also preserved and reflected.

Therefore every atomic formula, including equality, has the same truth value on \(\bar c\) and on \(\eta(\bar c)\). \(\square\)

### Lemma 4.2

Let \(\theta(\bar x,\bar p)\) be any quantifier-free \(L_{\omega_1\omega}\)-formula with parameters \(\bar p\) from \(p\). Then for every tuple \(\bar c\),
\[
\mathcal A\models \theta(\bar c,\bar p)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\mathcal A\models \theta(\eta(\bar c),\bar p).
\]

#### Proof

By Lemma 4.1, the equivalence holds for atomic formulas. Since \(\eta\) fixes every element of \(p\), it also holds for atomic formulas involving parameters from \(p\).

The general quantifier-free case follows by induction on formula complexity. Negation preserves equivalence, and so do arbitrary countable conjunctions and disjunctions. \(\square\)

### Lemma 4.3

Let \(\varphi(x,y)\) be any existential formula with parameters from \(p\). If
\[
\mathcal A\models \varphi(r_0,r_1),
\]
then
\[
\mathcal A\models \varphi(s_0,r_1).
\]

#### Proof

Write
\[
\varphi(x,y)\equiv \exists \bar z\,\theta(x,y,\bar z,\bar p),
\]
where \(\bar p\) is a tuple from \(p\), \(\bar z\) is finite, and \(\theta\) is quantifier-free.

Assume
\[
\mathcal A\models \varphi(r_0,r_1).
\]
Choose witnesses \(\bar c\) such that
\[
\mathcal A\models \theta(r_0,r_1,\bar c,\bar p).
\]
By Lemma 4.2,
\[
\mathcal A\models \theta(\eta(r_0),\eta(r_1),\eta(\bar c),\bar p).
\]
Since
\[
\eta(r_0)=s_0
\quad\text{and}\quad
\eta(r_1)=r_1,
\]
we get
\[
\mathcal A\models \theta(s_0,r_1,\eta(\bar c),\bar p).
\]
Therefore
\[
\mathcal A\models \varphi(s_0,r_1).
\]
\(\square\)

### 4.2. No \(\Sigma^{in}_1\)-definition of \(\pi\)

Suppose, toward a contradiction, that the graph of \(\pi\) were \(\Sigma^{in}_1\)-definable over parameters from \(p\). Then there would be a countable family of existential formulas
\[
(\varphi_i(x,y))_{i\in\omega}
\]
with parameters from \(p\) such that for all \(x,y\in A\),
\[
\pi(x)=y
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\mathcal A\models \varphi_i(x,y)\text{ for some }i\in\omega.
\]

Since
\[
\pi(r_0)=r_1,
\]
there is some \(i\) such that
\[
\mathcal A\models \varphi_i(r_0,r_1).
\]
By Lemma 4.3,
\[
\mathcal A\models \varphi_i(s_0,r_1).
\]
Hence the alleged \(\Sigma^{in}_1\)-definition would imply
\[
\pi(s_0)=r_1.
\]
But
\[
\pi(s_0)=v_{l,1,1}\neq v_{l,1,0}=r_1.
\]
This contradiction shows that \(\pi\) is not \(\Sigma^{in}_1\)-definable over the parameter set
\[
p=\operatorname{rng}(\bar a)\cup \pi[\operatorname{rng}(\bar a)].
\]

Since \(\bar a\) was arbitrary, the required nondefinability property holds for every finite tuple of parameters.

---

## Conclusion

The structure \(\mathcal A\) is computable, has finite relational language, and satisfies:

- every automorphism of every copy \(\mathcal B\cong\mathcal A\) is computable relative to \(D(\mathcal B)\), so the cone base can be taken to be \(\emptyset\);
- for every finite tuple \(\bar a\), there is an automorphism \(\pi\) of \(\mathcal A\) that is not \(\Sigma^{in}_1\)-definable using parameters from \(\bar a\cup\pi(\bar a)\).

Thus the answer to the original question is yes. \(\square\)

# Original Problem (for reference; restate as the headline theorem)
We consider infinite countable first-order structures, their automorphism groups and the descriptive aspects of their representations. A representation $\A$ of such a structure is an instance of its isomorphism type in which the universe or domain of the structure is the set of natural numbers $\N$ and the constants, functions and relations of the structure are elements of $\N$, multi-variable functions from $\N$ to $\N$ and multi-place relations on $\N$, respectively.   Similarly, an automorphism of $\A$ or an isomorphism from $\A$ to $\B$ is a bijective function from $\N$ to $\N$ that preserves the evaluation of all constants, functions and relations.  We let $\AUT(\A)$ denote the automorphism group of $\A$.

\begin{definition}\label{1.1}
  \begin{itemize}
  \item     $\A$ is \emph{computably $\AUT$-countable} if every automorphism of $\A$ is computable relative to $\A$.  
  \item  $\A$ is \emph{computably $\AUT$-countable on a cone} if there is a $C\subset\N$ such that whenever $\B$ is isomorphic to $\A$, every automorphism of $\B$ is computable relative to $\B\oplus C$.
  \end{itemize}
\end{definition}


\begin{definition}
  A function $f:\A\to\A$ is \emph{$\Sigma^{in}_1$-definable} in $\A$ relative to parameters
  $\bar{a}=(a_1,\dots,a_n)$ from $\A$ if there is a family of existential formulas $(\phi_i)_{i\in\N}$ such that for all $x$ and $y$ in $\A$, $f(x)=y$ if and only if there is an $i\in\N$ such that $\A\models\phi_i(\bar{a},x,y)$.
\end{definition}

\begin{question}\label{sec:question}\label{1.2}
  Is there a countable computably-represented structure $\A$ such that $\A$ is computably $\AUT$-countable on a cone and such that for any finite  set of parameters $\bar{a}$ in $\A$ there is an automorphism $\pi$ of $\A$ such that $\pi$ is not $\Sigma^{in}_1$-definable in $\A$ relative to the parameters $\bar{a}\cup \pi(\bar{a}),$ where $\pi(\bar{a})$ is the image of $\bar{a}$ under $\pi$.
\end{question}


# Source Document
## A self-contained positive solution

We prove a slightly stronger statement than the one asked for: the cone base can be taken to be the empty set, and the language can be finite and relational. The proof is self-contained; all facts used below are proved in the text.

### Theorem

There is a computable structure \(\mathcal A\) with domain \(\omega\) in a finite relational language such that:

1. For every copy \(\mathcal B\cong\mathcal A\) with domain \(\omega\), every automorphism of \(\mathcal B\) is computable relative to the atomic diagram of \(\mathcal B\). Thus \(\mathcal A\) is computably \(\operatorname{AUT}\)-countable on a cone, with cone base \(\emptyset\).

2. For every finite tuple \(\bar a\) from \(\mathcal A\), there is an automorphism \(\pi\in\operatorname{Aut}(\mathcal A)\) such that \(\pi\) is not \(\Sigma^{in}_1\)-definable in \(\mathcal A\) using parameters from
   \[
   \operatorname{rng}(\bar a)\cup \pi[\operatorname{rng}(\bar a)].
   \]

We use the following convention about formulas. An existential formula means a formula of the form \(\exists \bar z\,\theta\), where \(\bar z\) is finite and \(\theta\) is quantifier-free. The nondefinability argument below works even if \(\theta\) is allowed to be an infinitary quantifier-free \(L_{\omega_1\omega}\)-formula, built from atomic formulas by negation and countable conjunctions and disjunctions. Therefore it applies in particular to the narrower first-order reading of the definition in the problem.

---

## 1. Construction of the computable structure

Fix a computable pairing function \(\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle:\omega^2\to\omega\) with computable projections.

For each \(e\in\omega\), let
\[
F_e=\{i\in\omega:\text{the }i\text{th binary digit of }e\text{ is }1\}.
\]
Thus \(F_e\) is a finite subset of \(\omega\), and every finite subset of \(\omega\) is equal to \(F_e\) for a unique \(e\).

We divide \(\omega\) into three computable sorts. For \(l,e,k\in\omega\) and \(b\in\{0,1\}\), define
\[
\lambda_l=3l,\qquad u_e=3e+1,\qquad v_{l,b,k}=3\langle l,2k+b\rangle+2.
\]
Every natural number is uniquely of one of these three forms.

The language is the finite relational language
\[
\tau=\{L,U,V,N,I,Q,S,T\},
\]
where \(L,U,V\) are unary, \(N,I\) are binary, and \(Q,S,T\) are ternary.

Intuitively:

- \(L=\{\lambda_l:l\in\omega\}\) is a rigid label ray.
- \(U=\{u_e:e\in\omega\}\) codes finite subsets of the label set.
- Over each label \(l\), the \(V\)-sort contains two successor rays
  \[
  R_{l,0}=\{v_{l,0,k}:k\in\omega\},\qquad R_{l,1}=\{v_{l,1,k}:k\in\omega\}.
  \]
  Put
  \[
  P_l=R_{l,0}\cup R_{l,1}.
  \]

Define the relations of \(\mathcal A\) as follows.

1. Sort predicates:
   \[
   L(\lambda_l),\qquad U(u_e),\qquad V(v_{l,b,k})
   \]
   hold for all relevant indices, and the sort predicates are false elsewhere.

2. The label successor relation:
   \[
   N(\lambda_l,\lambda_m)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad m=l+1.
   \]

3. The incidence relation between labels and \(V\)-gadgets:
   \[
   I(\lambda_l,v_{m,b,k})\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad l=m.
   \]

4. The finite-set toggle relation:
   \[
   Q(\lambda_l,u_e,u_{e'})\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad F_{e'}=F_e\triangle\{l\},
   \]
   where \(\triangle\) denotes symmetric difference.

5. The successor relation on the two rays over each label:
   \[
   S(\lambda_l,v_{l,b,k},v_{l,b,k+1})
   \]
   holds for all \(l,b,k\), and \(S\) is false on all other triples.

6. The side-selection relation:
   \[
   T(\lambda_l,u_e,v_{m,b,k})
   \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
   m=l\text{ and }b=\chi_{F_e}(l),
   \]
   where \(\chi_{F_e}(l)=1\) if \(l\in F_e\), and \(\chi_{F_e}(l)=0\) otherwise.

All relations are computable. Indeed, the three sorts are computable from congruence modulo \(3\), the coordinates of \(v_{l,b,k}\) are computably recoverable using the pairing projections and parity, and the finite-set operations \(F_e\mapsto F_e\triangle\{l\}\) and \(l\in F_e\) are computable from binary notation. Therefore \(\mathcal A\) is a computable structure with domain \(\omega\).

---

## 2. Classification of the automorphisms of \(\mathcal A\)

For every finite set \(F\subseteq\omega\), define a map \(\alpha_F:A\to A\) by
\[
\alpha_F(\lambda_l)=\lambda_l,
\]
\[
\alpha_F(u_e)=u_{e'},
\quad\text{where }F_{e'}=F_e\triangle F,
\]
and
\[
\alpha_F(v_{l,b,k})=v_{l,b\oplus\chi_F(l),k},
\]
where \(\oplus\) is addition modulo \(2\).

Thus \(\alpha_F\) fixes all labels, toggles the coded finite set on \(U\) by symmetric difference with \(F\), and swaps the two rays over exactly those labels \(l\in F\).

### Lemma 2.1

For every finite \(F\subseteq\omega\), \(\alpha_F\in\operatorname{Aut}(\mathcal A)\).

#### Proof

The map \(\alpha_F\) is a bijection, and in fact an involution. It clearly preserves the three sorts \(L,U,V\). Since all labels are fixed, \(N\) is preserved.

The relation \(I\) is preserved because \(\alpha_F\) does not change the label coordinate of a \(V\)-element.

The relation \(S\) is preserved because, over a fixed label \(l\), \(\alpha_F\) either fixes both rays pointwise level-by-level or swaps them level-by-level. In either case,
\[
S(\lambda_l,v_{l,b,k},v_{l,b,k+1})
\]
holds if and only if
\[
S(\lambda_l,v_{l,b\oplus\chi_F(l),k},v_{l,b\oplus\chi_F(l),k+1})
\]
holds.

For \(Q\), suppose
\[
Q(\lambda_l,u_e,u_{e'})
\]
holds. Then
\[
F_{e'}=F_e\triangle\{l\}.
\]
After applying \(\alpha_F\), the two \(U\)-elements code
\[
F_e\triangle F
\quad\text{and}\quad
F_{e'}\triangle F.
\]
But
\[
F_{e'}\triangle F
=
(F_e\triangle\{l\})\triangle F
=
(F_e\triangle F)\triangle\{l\}.
\]
Hence \(Q\) is preserved.

For \(T\), suppose
\[
T(\lambda_l,u_e,v_{m,b,k})
\]
holds. Then \(m=l\) and \(b=\chi_{F_e}(l)\). After applying \(\alpha_F\), the \(U\)-element codes \(F_e\triangle F\), and the side of the \(V\)-element becomes
\[
b\oplus\chi_F(l)
=
\chi_{F_e}(l)\oplus\chi_F(l)
=
\chi_{F_e\triangle F}(l).
\]
Thus \(T\) is preserved. Since \(\alpha_F\) is a bijection and an involution, preservation is equivalent to preservation and reflection. Therefore \(\alpha_F\) is an automorphism. \(\square\)

### Lemma 2.2

Every automorphism of \(\mathcal A\) is equal to \(\alpha_F\) for a unique finite set \(F\subseteq\omega\).

#### Proof

Let \(\beta\in\operatorname{Aut}(\mathcal A)\).

First, \(\beta\) fixes every label. The element \(\lambda_0\) is the unique \(L\)-element with no \(N\)-predecessor. Hence \(\beta(\lambda_0)=\lambda_0\). If \(\beta(\lambda_l)=\lambda_l\), then \(\lambda_{l+1}\) is the unique \(N\)-successor of \(\lambda_l\), so \(\beta(\lambda_{l+1})=\lambda_{l+1}\). By induction,
\[
\beta(\lambda_l)=\lambda_l
\]
for every \(l\).

Next consider the action on \(U\). For each \(l\), let \(\tau_l:U\to U\) be the toggle map
\[
\tau_l(u_e)=u_{e'},
\quad\text{where }F_{e'}=F_e\triangle\{l\}.
\]
The relation \(Q\) says exactly that
\[
Q(\lambda_l,u,\tau_l(u))
\]
holds. Since \(\beta\) fixes \(\lambda_l\) and preserves \(Q\), we have
\[
\beta(\tau_l(u))=\tau_l(\beta(u))
\]
for every \(u\in U\).

Let
\[
\beta(u_0)=u_d,
\]
and put
\[
F=F_d.
\]
If \(u_e\in U\), then \(F_e\) is finite, say
\[
F_e=\{l_0,\dots,l_{n-1}\}.
\]
Then
\[
u_e=\tau_{l_0}\cdots\tau_{l_{n-1}}(u_0).
\]
Using the commutation relation above,
\[
\beta(u_e)
=
\tau_{l_0}\cdots\tau_{l_{n-1}}(\beta(u_0))
=
\tau_{l_0}\cdots\tau_{l_{n-1}}(u_d).
\]
Therefore \(\beta(u_e)=u_{e'}\), where
\[
F_{e'}=F_e\triangle F.
\]

It remains to analyze \(V\). Since \(\beta\) fixes every \(\lambda_l\) and preserves \(I\), it maps each gadget
\[
P_l=R_{l,0}\cup R_{l,1}
\]
onto itself. On \(P_l\), the relation \(S(\lambda_l,\cdot,\cdot)\) is exactly two disjoint directed \(\omega\)-rays:
\[
R_{l,0}:v_{l,0,0}\to v_{l,0,1}\to v_{l,0,2}\to\cdots,
\]
\[
R_{l,1}:v_{l,1,0}\to v_{l,1,1}\to v_{l,1,2}\to\cdots.
\]
The roots \(v_{l,0,0}\) and \(v_{l,1,0}\) are precisely the two elements of \(P_l\) with no \(S(\lambda_l)\)-predecessor. Hence \(\beta\) either fixes the two rays or swaps them. Once the image of a root is chosen, the rest of the ray is forced by preservation of the unique successor relation. Thus for each \(l\), there is some \(\epsilon_l\in\{0,1\}\) such that
\[
\beta(v_{l,b,k})=v_{l,b\oplus\epsilon_l,k}
\]
for all \(b,k\).

We now determine \(\epsilon_l\). Since \(u_0\) codes the empty set, we have
\[
T(\lambda_l,u_0,v_{l,0,0})
\]
for every \(l\). Applying \(\beta\), using \(\beta(\lambda_l)=\lambda_l\) and \(\beta(u_0)=u_d\), gives
\[
T(\lambda_l,u_d,v_{l,\epsilon_l,0}).
\]
By the definition of \(T\), this means
\[
\epsilon_l=\chi_{F_d}(l)=\chi_F(l).
\]
Therefore
\[
\beta(v_{l,b,k})=v_{l,b\oplus\chi_F(l),k}.
\]

Combining the descriptions on \(L,U,V\), we get
\[
\beta=\alpha_F.
\]
The set \(F\) is unique because \(F=F_d\), where \(\beta(u_0)=u_d\). \(\square\)

Thus
\[
\operatorname{Aut}(\mathcal A)=\{\alpha_F:F\subseteq\omega\text{ finite}\}.
\]
In particular, \(\operatorname{Aut}(\mathcal A)\) is countable, and in the standard computable presentation every automorphism is computable.

---

## 3. Automorphisms of arbitrary copies are computable from the copy

We now prove the stronger “on a cone” statement with cone base \(\emptyset\).

Let \(\mathcal B\cong\mathcal A\) be any copy with domain \(\omega\), and let
\[
\rho\in\operatorname{Aut}(\mathcal B).
\]
Write \(D(\mathcal B)\) for the complete atomic diagram of \(\mathcal B\), equivalently, in this finite relational language, the join of the characteristic functions of the finitely many relations of \(\mathcal B\).

Choose some isomorphism
\[
h:\mathcal A\to\mathcal B.
\]
Then
\[
h^{-1}\rho h\in\operatorname{Aut}(\mathcal A),
\]
so by Lemma 2.2, there is a finite set \(F\subseteq\omega\) such that
\[
h^{-1}\rho h=\alpha_F.
\]

Let
\[
M=\{h(\lambda_l):l\in F\}.
\]
This is the finite set of labels in \(\mathcal B\) whose two \(S\)-rays are swapped by \(\rho\).

For each \(f=h(\lambda_l)\in M\), fix and hardwire into the program the two roots
\[
r_f^0=h(v_{l,0,0}),\qquad r_f^1=h(v_{l,1,0})
\]
of the two rays over \(f\).

This finite hardwiring is legitimate: to say \(\rho\leq_T D(\mathcal B)\) means that there exists an oracle program computing \(\rho\) from \(D(\mathcal B)\), and the program index may depend on the particular pair \((\mathcal B,\rho)\). Uniformity in \(\rho\) is not part of the definition.

We now describe the oracle computation of \(\rho(x)\) from \(D(\mathcal B)\).

### Case 1: \(x\in L^\mathcal B\)

Query \(L^\mathcal B(x)\). If true, output
\[
x.
\]
This is correct because every automorphism fixes the label ray pointwise.

### Case 2: \(x\in U^\mathcal B\)

If \(U^\mathcal B(x)\), set \(y=x\). For each hardwired support label \(f\in M\), search through \(z=0,1,2,\dots\) until finding the unique \(z\) such that
\[
\mathcal B\models Q(f,y,z).
\]
Then replace \(y\) by \(z\), and continue to the next \(f\in M\). After all support labels have been used, output the resulting \(y\).

This computation halts because, in every copy of \(\mathcal A\), for each label \(f\) and each \(U\)-element \(y\), there is a unique \(z\) satisfying \(Q(f,y,z)\). The effect is to toggle precisely the finitely many labels in \(M\), which is exactly the action of \(\rho\) on \(U^\mathcal B\).

### Case 3: \(x\in V^\mathcal B\)

If \(V^\mathcal B(x)\), first check the finitely many labels \(f\in M\).

If no \(f\in M\) satisfies
\[
\mathcal B\models I(f,x),
\]
then output
\[
x.
\]
This is correct because \(\rho\) fixes every \(V\)-gadget whose label is not in the finite support set \(M\).

Otherwise, there is a unique \(f\in M\) such that
\[
\mathcal B\models I(f,x).
\]
The element \(x\) lies on one of the two rays over \(f\), and \(\rho\) swaps those two rays level-by-level.

Start with the hardwired roots
\[
y_0=r_f^0,\qquad y_1=r_f^1.
\]
Proceed in stages:

- If \(x=y_0\), output \(y_1\).
- If \(x=y_1\), output \(y_0\).
- Otherwise, search for the unique successors \(y_0'\) and \(y_1'\) satisfying
  \[
  \mathcal B\models S(f,y_0,y_0')
  \]
  and
  \[
  \mathcal B\models S(f,y_1,y_1').
  \]
  Replace \(y_0,y_1\) by \(y_0',y_1'\), and repeat.

This halts because every element of the gadget over \(f\) is at some finite level on exactly one of the two rays. The output is the element at the same level on the other ray, which is exactly \(\rho(x)\).

Thus for every copy \(\mathcal B\cong\mathcal A\) and every automorphism \(\rho\in\operatorname{Aut}(\mathcal B)\),
\[
\rho\leq_T D(\mathcal B).
\]
Therefore \(\mathcal A\) is computably \(\operatorname{AUT}\)-countable on a cone with cone base \(\emptyset\).

---

## 4. The nondefinability argument

Let \(\bar a\) be any finite tuple from \(\mathcal A\). We will find an automorphism \(\pi\) such that \(\pi\) is not \(\Sigma^{in}_1\)-definable using parameters from
\[
\operatorname{rng}(\bar a)\cup \pi[\operatorname{rng}(\bar a)].
\]

Only finitely many entries of \(\bar a\) can lie in the \(V\)-sort, and each \(V\)-element lies in exactly one gadget \(P_l\). Choose \(l\in\omega\) such that no entry of \(\bar a\) lies in \(P_l\).

Let
\[
\pi=\alpha_{\{l\}}.
\]
Thus \(\pi\) swaps the two rays over label \(l\), toggles the \(l\)th bit on \(U\), and fixes every \(P_m\) with \(m\neq l\).

Because no entry of \(\bar a\) lies in \(P_l\), and because \(\pi\) sends elements of \(P_m\) to \(P_m\), the finite parameter set
\[
p=\operatorname{rng}(\bar a)\cup \pi[\operatorname{rng}(\bar a)]
\]
contains no element of \(P_l\). It may contain labels, including possibly \(\lambda_l\), and it may contain \(U\)-elements. That will not matter.

Now set
\[
r_0=v_{l,0,0},\qquad r_1=v_{l,1,0},\qquad s_0=v_{l,0,1}.
\]
Then
\[
\pi(r_0)=r_1,
\]
but
\[
\pi(s_0)=v_{l,1,1}\neq r_1.
\]

We will show that every existential formula over parameters from \(p\) that is true of the genuine graph pair \((r_0,r_1)\) is also true of the false pair \((s_0,r_1)\). This will rule out every countable disjunction of existential formulas as a definition of the graph of \(\pi\).

### 4.1. The shift map

Define a map \(\eta:A\to A\) by shifting the \(0\)-side ray over \(l\) forward one step and fixing everything else:
\[
\eta(v_{l,0,k})=v_{l,0,k+1}
\]
for all \(k\), and
\[
\eta(x)=x
\]
for all other \(x\in A\).

Then \(\eta\) is injective, fixes every element of \(p\), sends
\[
\eta(r_0)=s_0,
\]
and fixes
\[
\eta(r_1)=r_1.
\]

The key point is that \(\eta\) preserves and reflects all atomic facts.

### Lemma 4.1

For every atomic formula \(\theta(\bar x)\), allowing equality, and every tuple \(\bar c\) from \(A\),
\[
\mathcal A\models \theta(\bar c)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\mathcal A\models \theta(\eta(\bar c)).
\]

#### Proof

Equality is preserved and reflected because \(\eta\) is injective.

The unary predicates \(L,U,V\) are preserved and reflected because \(\eta\) preserves the three sorts.

The relation \(N\) is preserved and reflected because \(\eta\) fixes all label elements.

The relation \(Q\) is preserved and reflected because \(\eta\) fixes all label elements and all \(U\)-elements.

The relation \(I\) is preserved and reflected because \(\eta\) never changes the label coordinate of a \(V\)-element.

The relation \(T\) is preserved and reflected because \(T(\lambda_m,u_e,v_{m,b,k})\) depends only on the label \(m\), the \(U\)-element \(u_e\), and the side \(b\), not on the level \(k\). The map \(\eta\) fixes labels and \(U\)-elements and, on \(V\), does not change the label coordinate or the side.

Finally consider \(S\). On every \(S\)-ray except \(R_{l,0}\), the map \(\eta\) is the identity. On \(R_{l,0}\), it is the successor map
\[
v_{l,0,k}\mapsto v_{l,0,k+1}.
\]
For \(k,j\in\omega\),
\[
S(\lambda_l,v_{l,0,k},v_{l,0,j})
\]
holds if and only if \(j=k+1\), and this is equivalent to
\[
S(\lambda_l,v_{l,0,k+1},v_{l,0,j+1}).
\]
There are no \(S\)-edges between distinct rays. Hence \(S\) is also preserved and reflected.

Therefore every atomic formula, including equality, has the same truth value on \(\bar c\) and on \(\eta(\bar c)\). \(\square\)

### Lemma 4.2

Let \(\theta(\bar x,\bar p)\) be any quantifier-free \(L_{\omega_1\omega}\)-formula with parameters \(\bar p\) from \(p\). Then for every tuple \(\bar c\),
\[
\mathcal A\models \theta(\bar c,\bar p)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\mathcal A\models \theta(\eta(\bar c),\bar p).
\]

#### Proof

By Lemma 4.1, the equivalence holds for atomic formulas. Since \(\eta\) fixes every element of \(p\), it also holds for atomic formulas involving parameters from \(p\).

The general quantifier-free case follows by induction on formula complexity. Negation preserves equivalence, and so do arbitrary countable conjunctions and disjunctions. \(\square\)

### Lemma 4.3

Let \(\varphi(x,y)\) be any existential formula with parameters from \(p\). If
\[
\mathcal A\models \varphi(r_0,r_1),
\]
then
\[
\mathcal A\models \varphi(s_0,r_1).
\]

#### Proof

Write
\[
\varphi(x,y)\equiv \exists \bar z\,\theta(x,y,\bar z,\bar p),
\]
where \(\bar p\) is a tuple from \(p\), \(\bar z\) is finite, and \(\theta\) is quantifier-free.

Assume
\[
\mathcal A\models \varphi(r_0,r_1).
\]
Choose witnesses \(\bar c\) such that
\[
\mathcal A\models \theta(r_0,r_1,\bar c,\bar p).
\]
By Lemma 4.2,
\[
\mathcal A\models \theta(\eta(r_0),\eta(r_1),\eta(\bar c),\bar p).
\]
Since
\[
\eta(r_0)=s_0
\quad\text{and}\quad
\eta(r_1)=r_1,
\]
we get
\[
\mathcal A\models \theta(s_0,r_1,\eta(\bar c),\bar p).
\]
Therefore
\[
\mathcal A\models \varphi(s_0,r_1).
\]
\(\square\)

### 4.2. No \(\Sigma^{in}_1\)-definition of \(\pi\)

Suppose, toward a contradiction, that the graph of \(\pi\) were \(\Sigma^{in}_1\)-definable over parameters from \(p\). Then there would be a countable family of existential formulas
\[
(\varphi_i(x,y))_{i\in\omega}
\]
with parameters from \(p\) such that for all \(x,y\in A\),
\[
\pi(x)=y
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\mathcal A\models \varphi_i(x,y)\text{ for some }i\in\omega.
\]

Since
\[
\pi(r_0)=r_1,
\]
there is some \(i\) such that
\[
\mathcal A\models \varphi_i(r_0,r_1).
\]
By Lemma 4.3,
\[
\mathcal A\models \varphi_i(s_0,r_1).
\]
Hence the alleged \(\Sigma^{in}_1\)-definition would imply
\[
\pi(s_0)=r_1.
\]
But
\[
\pi(s_0)=v_{l,1,1}\neq v_{l,1,0}=r_1.
\]
This contradiction shows that \(\pi\) is not \(\Sigma^{in}_1\)-definable over the parameter set
\[
p=\operatorname{rng}(\bar a)\cup \pi[\operatorname{rng}(\bar a)].
\]

Since \(\bar a\) was arbitrary, the required nondefinability property holds for every finite tuple of parameters.

---

## Conclusion

The structure \(\mathcal A\) is computable, has finite relational language, and satisfies:

- every automorphism of every copy \(\mathcal B\cong\mathcal A\) is computable relative to \(D(\mathcal B)\), so the cone base can be taken to be \(\emptyset\);
- for every finite tuple \(\bar a\), there is an automorphism \(\pi\) of \(\mathcal A\) that is not \(\Sigma^{in}_1\)-definable using parameters from \(\bar a\cup\pi(\bar a)\).

Thus the answer to the original question is yes. \(\square\)
